[Hinduism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") is the prominent religion of Ranchi followed by 64.31% of the population. [Islam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islam "Islam") is the second most followed religion in the city by 16.42% of the people. Minorities are [Christians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christians "Christians") 8.52%, [Sikhs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") 0.39%, [Jains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jains "Jains") 0.22%, [Buddhists](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buddhists "Buddhists") 0.06%, those that did not state a religion are 0.35%, and [tribal religion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tribal_religions_in_India "Tribal religions in India")– primarily the [Sarnaists](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarnaism "Sarnaism") are 9.72%.[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-Religion-48)
## Governance and politics
### Civic administration
The municipal corporation covers an area of 175 km2 (68 sq mi) and is divided into 55 administrative wards, each represented by an elected corporator.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-RMC-1)
At the time of the creation of the state of Jharkhand in 2000, the Bihar Municipal Act, 1922 was adapted as the [Jharkhand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand") Municipal Act, 2000 to govern all ULBs in the new state. Ranchi Municipal Corporation was governed by its own municipal act, the Ranchi Municipal Corporation Act, 2001. With the enactment of the Jharkhand Municipal Act 2011, these two ceased to be in effect and the 2011 became the only governing legislature for all city governments in Jharkhand.[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-49)
Under section 26 of the Jharkhand Municipal Act, 2011, the positions of mayor and deputy mayor are elected directly i.e. the position holders are voted in by the people themselves.[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-:2-50) The last municipal election was in 2018 with a voter turnout of 49.3%, up from 38% in the last election in 2013.[[51]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-51)[[52]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-52) Asha Lakra and Sanjiv Vijayawargia – both from the [Bharatiya Janta Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party") – won the mayoral and deputy mayoral seats.[[53]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-:1-53) The 2018 municipal election was the first time the mayoral and deputy mayoral candidates in Jharkhand could use political party symbols, while the candidates for councilor seats continued to use symbols allotted to them by the Jharkhand State Election Commission.[[53]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-:1-53)[[54]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-54) The next municipal election will take place in 2023.[[55]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-:0-55)
The Jharkhand Municipal Act, 2011 mandates the establishment of ward committees for each ward in the municipality.[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-:2-50) Though ward committees have been formed in Ranchi, they are not active.[[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-56)
### Lok Sabha and Vidhan Sabha Constituencies
Ranchi city is part of the [Ranchi Lok Sabha Constituency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi_\(Lok_Sabha_constituency\) "Ranchi \(Lok Sabha constituency\)"). [Sanjay Seth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sanjay_Seth_\(Jharkhand_politician\) "Sanjay Seth \(Jharkhand politician\)") won the seat in the [2019 General Election](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2019_Indian_general_election "2019 Indian general election") and represents this constituency at the [Lok Sabha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lok_Sabha "Lok Sabha") as a [Member of Parliament](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Member_of_parliament,_Lok_Sabha "Member of parliament, Lok Sabha").[[57]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-57) Ranchi is part of the [Ranchi Vidhan Sabha Constituency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi_\(Vidhan_Sabha_constituency\) "Ranchi \(Vidhan Sabha constituency\)") and the representative [Member of Legislative Assembly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Member_of_the_Legislative_Assembly_\(India\) "Member of the Legislative Assembly \(India\)") at the [Jharkhand Vidhan Sabha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand_Legislative_Assembly "Jharkhand Legislative Assembly") is [Chandreshwar Prasad Singh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chandreshwar_Prasad_Singh "Chandreshwar Prasad Singh"). Singh has held the post since 2000, with the [last Jharkhand Vidhan Sabha election](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2019_Jharkhand_Legislative_Assembly_election "2019 Jharkhand Legislative Assembly election") being in 2019. Both politicians belong to the [Bharatiya Janata Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party").[[58]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-58)
## Public utilities
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Ranchi_Airport_Night_View.jpg/250px-Ranchi_Airport_Night_View.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Ranchi_Airport_Night_View.jpg)[Birsa Munda Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birsa_Munda_Airport "Birsa Munda Airport")[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Ranchi_Junction_railway_station_entrance_at_night.jpg/250px-Ranchi_Junction_railway_station_entrance_at_night.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Ranchi_Junction_railway_station_entrance_at_night.jpg)[Ranchi Junction railway station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi_Junction_railway_station "Ranchi Junction railway station")
The various infrastructure and civic services demands of the city are met by different entities, including Ranchi Municipal Corporation, state government departments, and parastatal organisations. The Ranchi Master Plan 2037 was created by a private consultant firm under the clientship of the Ranchi Municipal Corporation, Urban Development and Housing Department, and Ranchi Regional Development Authority.[[59]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-59)[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-60) Roads in Ranchi are developed and maintained by the municipal corporation, [Jharkhand Government](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Government_of_Jharkhand "Government of Jharkhand"), Ranchi Regional Development Authority, and [National Highways Authority of India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Highways_Authority_of_India "National Highways Authority of India").[[61]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-61) The Water Supply Section of the municipal corporation is responsible for issuing water connections, maintaining hand pumps in wards, collecting water usage charges and fines, whereas the state government Drinking Water Supply and Sewerage Department supplies the water.[[62]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-62)[[63]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-63) Ranchi is served by [Dhurwa Dam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dhurwa_Dam "Dhurwa Dam"). The Health Department of the corporation is responsible for the [Solid Waste Management](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waste_management "Waste management") of the city. RMC operates buses in the city and has outsourced this to a private agency.[[64]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-64)
## Transport
  * **Airways** - [Birsa Munda Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birsa_Munda_Airport "Birsa Munda Airport") is the busiest airport in the state which connects direct flights from major cities of india like [New Delhi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi"), [Mumbai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"), [Kolkata](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata"), [Hyderabad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hyderabad "Hyderabad"), [Chennai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chennai "Chennai"), [Bengaluru](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bengaluru "Bengaluru"), [Lucknow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucknow "Lucknow"), [Pune](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pune "Pune") and many more cities also a new terminal of airport is purposed.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]
  * **Railways** - Ranchi has four railway stations which connects direct trains from ranchi to major capital cities of india which includes [New Delhi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi"), [Mumbai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"), [Kolkata](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata"), [Chennai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chennai "Chennai"), [Bengaluru](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bengaluru "Bengaluru"), [Hyderabad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hyderabad "Hyderabad"), [Patna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patna "Patna"), and major of the cities of india. Their Names are 1. [Ranchi railway station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi_railway_station "Ranchi railway station") 2. [Hatia railway station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hatia_railway_station "Hatia railway station") 3. [Muri Junction railway station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muri_Junction_railway_station "Muri Junction railway station") 4. [Tatisilwai railway station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tatisilwai_railway_station "Tatisilwai railway station").
  * **Roadways** - Ranchi is well connected with all the major cities of india as and its neighbour states with [National Highways Authority of India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Highways_Authority_of_India "National Highways Authority of India") all districts of Jharkhand is well connected through road network.
  * **Buses** - There are two bus terminals Khadgarah Bus Station and Railway Bus Terminal majority of buses operate from these terminals and several bus stops are built within the city.


## Economy
Ranchi is one of the major industrial cities of [Eastern India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/East_India "East India") and located in the region called 'Ruhr of India' ([Chota Nagpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chota_Nagpur_Plateau "Chota Nagpur Plateau")) making it an ideal place for mineral based industries. Ranchi has presence of many well-known government and private organisations in the vicinity of city. [SAIL-R&D](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steel_Authority_of_India "Steel Authority of India"), [MECON.Ltd](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MECON_\(company\) "MECON \(company\)"), [Garden Reach Shipbuilders & Engineers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Garden_Reach_Shipbuilders_%26_Engineers "Garden Reach Shipbuilders & Engineers") (Marine Diesel Engine Project), [Central Coalfields](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Coalfields "Central Coalfields"), CMPDI.Ltd, Subarnarekha Hydel Project ([JSEB](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand_State_Electricity_Board "Jharkhand State Electricity Board")), Usha Martin.Ltd (Wire Rope Division),[[65]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-65) [DVC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Damodar_Valley_Corporation "Damodar Valley Corporation"), [STPI-Ranchi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software_Technology_Parks_of_India "Software Technology Parks of India"),[[66]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-66) [Heavy Engineering Corp.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heavy_Engineering_Corporation "Heavy Engineering Corporation"), Palriwal Industries, Pensol India, Waxpol Industries, Jharkhand Mega Food Park etc. are the prominent industries here. 
The Federation of Jharkhand Chamber of Commerce & Industries (FJCCI),[[67]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-67) [ASSOCHAM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ASSOCHAM "ASSOCHAM"), [MSME-DI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Micro,_Small_and_Medium_Enterprises_Development_Act,_2006 "Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises Development Act, 2006") and JIADA[[68]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-68) are major apex industrial bodies present here. JIADA consists many industrial areas in Ranchi, e.g. – Kokar, [Tatisilwai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tati,_Ranchi "Tati, Ranchi"), [Namkum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Namkum "Namkum") etc. are among them. 
As per a study done by ASSOCHAM in late 2010, Ranchi was the highest employment generating Tier-III cities in India followed by Mangalore and Mysore.[[69]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-69) [MECON Ltd Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MECON_\(company\) "MECON \(company\)") has its headquarters in Ranchi at Ashok Nagar. Jharkhand Industrial Area Development Association has its office in [Namkum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Namkum "Namkum"). [Central Coalfields](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Coalfields "Central Coalfields") also has its headquarters in Ranchi. 
Ranchi houses a number of companies. Along with [Jamshedpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jamshedpur "Jamshedpur"), [Dhanbad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dhanbad "Dhanbad"), [Bokaro](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bokaro_Steel_City "Bokaro Steel City") and [Giridih](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Giridih "Giridih") it is one of the industrial hubs of the city.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_] Here are the following companies which are based in Ranchi and also headquartered in Ranchi itself:[Mecon limited](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mecon_limited "Mecon limited"), [Central Coalfields](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Coalfields "Central Coalfields"), [Heavy Engineering Corporation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heavy_Engineering_Corporation "Heavy Engineering Corporation"), [Central Mine Planning and Design Institute](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Mine_Planning_and_Design_Institute "Central Mine Planning and Design Institute"), [Jharkhand Rajya Gramin Bank](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand_Rajya_Gramin_Bank "Jharkhand Rajya Gramin Bank") and Dzinex Technology.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]
The main and major businesses here are located in [Lalpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lalpur,_Ranchi "Lalpur, Ranchi"), [Hindpiri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindpiri "Hindpiri"), Lower Bazaar, Upper Bazaar and [Doranda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Doranda "Doranda").[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]
[STPI Ranchi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software_Technology_Parks_of_India "Software Technology Parks of India") has set up its branch at [Namkum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Namkum "Namkum"), Ranchi. It is usually an [IT Park](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Business_park "Business park"), which houses many IT companies. It is being planned to build more parks like this. OFFCO Park is another park which is built here. It is also a large and famous business park. 
[Lalpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lalpur,_Ranchi "Lalpur, Ranchi"), Doranda and Hindpiri are the business districts and financial districts in the city and the state. This place houses several shopping malls. Whole of Ranchi has several shopping malls which include: [Nucleus Mall](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Nucleus_Mall&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nucleus Mall \(page does not exist\)"), Mall E Decor, [JD Hi-Street Mall](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=JD_Hi-Street_Mall&action=edit&redlink=1 "JD Hi-Street Mall \(page does not exist\)"), [Ranchi Central Mall](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ranchi_Central_Mall&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ranchi Central Mall \(page does not exist\)"), Laxmi Tower, Mall of Ranchi, City Center (Centre) Mall, Big Mall, Spring City Mall, Arakshan Shopping Mall, Shelter Mall, Ranchi Green City, Artic Mall, City Center or City Centre, City Mall and [Galaxia Mall](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Galaxia_Mall&action=edit&redlink=1 "Galaxia Mall \(page does not exist\)"). 
There are many other upcoming shopping malls and commercial projects

## Sports
Ranchi is a center for numerous sports activities, including cricket, hockey, football, and many others.[[73]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-73) The [34th National Games](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2011_National_Games_of_India "2011 National Games of India") were successfully held in Ranchi in February 2011. An [International Cricket stadium with an indoor stadium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSCA_International_Cricket_Stadium "JSCA International Cricket Stadium") and a practice ground has been constructed. So far, this stadium has hosted three One day International matches and one T20 International match. Apart from that, this stadium has hosted two [IPL 6](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IPL_6 "IPL 6") matches for [Kolkata Knight Riders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kolkata_Knight_Riders "Kolkata Knight Riders"), three champions league 2013 matches and Celebrity Cricket League matches for Bhojpuri Dabanggs. A tennis academy, which was inaugurated by [Sania Mirza](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sania_Mirza "Sania Mirza") and [Shoaib Malik](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shoaib_Malik "Shoaib Malik"), also runs beside the cricket stadium. Former Indian Cricket Team Captain [M.S. Dhoni](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MS_Dhoni "MS Dhoni") lives in Ranchi.[[74]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-74) The Ranchi franchise for Hockey India League was bought by Patel-Uniexcel Group and the team named the [Ranchi Rhinos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi_Rhinos "Ranchi Rhinos").[[75]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ranchi#cite_note-75)

**This article contains[Balinese alphabet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_alphabet "Balinese alphabet").** Without proper [rendering support](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Multilingual_support_\(East_Asian\)#Archaic_Hangul "Help:Multilingual support \(East Asian\)"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Specials_\(Unicode_block\)#Replacement_character "Specials \(Unicode block\)") instead of Balinese characters.
**Bali** (English: [/ˈbɑːli/](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/4/4e/LL-Q1860_%28eng%29-Vealhurl-Bali.wav/LL-Q1860_%28eng%29-Vealhurl-Bali.wav.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_\(eng\)-Vealhurl-Bali.wav "File:LL-Q1860 \(eng\)-Vealhurl-Bali.wav"); Indonesian: [['bali]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:IPA/Malay "Help:IPA/Malay"); [Balinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_language "Balinese language"): ᬩᬮᬶ) is an [Indonesian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") [island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Indonesia "List of islands of Indonesia") and [province](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Provinces_of_Indonesia "Provinces of Indonesia") and the westernmost of the [Lesser Sunda Islands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lesser_Sunda_Islands "Lesser Sunda Islands"). East of [Java](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Java "Java") and west of [Lombok](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lombok "Lombok"), the province includes the island of Bali and a few smaller offshore islands, notably [Nusa Penida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Penida "Nusa Penida"), [Nusa Lembongan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Lembongan "Nusa Lembongan"), and [Nusa Ceningan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Ceningan "Nusa Ceningan") to the southeast. The provincial capital, [Denpasar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Denpasar "Denpasar"),[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-15) is the [most populous city](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Indonesian_cities_by_population "List of Indonesian cities by population") in the Lesser Sunda Islands and the second-largest, after [Makassar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Makassar "Makassar"), in [Eastern Indonesia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eastern_Indonesia "Eastern Indonesia"). The [Denpasar metropolitan area](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Denpasar_metropolitan_area "Denpasar metropolitan area") is the extended metropolitan area around [Denpasar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Denpasar "Denpasar"). The upland town of [Ubud](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ubud "Ubud") in Greater Denpasar is considered as Bali's cultural centre. The province is Indonesia's main tourist destination, with a significant rise in [tourism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tourism_in_Bali "Tourism in Bali") since the 1980s, and has become the country's area of [overtourism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Overtourism "Overtourism").[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-16) Tourism-related business makes up 80% of the Bali economy.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-tourism-17)
Bali is the only [Hindu-majority province](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hinduism_in_Indonesia "Hinduism in Indonesia") in Indonesia, with 86.40% of the population adhering to [Balinese Hinduism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_Hinduism "Balinese Hinduism").[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-auto-9) It is renowned for its highly developed arts, including traditional and modern dance, sculpture, painting, leather, metalworking, and music. The Indonesian International Film Festival is held every year in Bali. Other international events that have been held in Bali include [Miss World 2013](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miss_World_2013 "Miss World 2013"), the 2018 [Annual Meetings of the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank Group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Annual_Meetings_of_the_International_Monetary_Fund_and_the_World_Bank_Group "Annual Meetings of the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank Group"), and the [2022 G20 summit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2022_G20_Bali_summit "2022 G20 Bali summit"). In March 2017, [Tripadvisor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tripadvisor "Tripadvisor") named Bali as the world's top destination in its Traveler's Choice award, which it earned once again in January 2021[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-18)[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-19) and 2026.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-20)[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-21)[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-22)
Bali is part of the [Coral Triangle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coral_Triangle "Coral Triangle"), an area with high diversity of marine species, mainly fish and turtles.[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-23) In this area alone, over 500 reef-building coral species can be found. For comparison, this is about seven times as many as in the entire [Caribbean](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean").[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-24) Bali is the home of the [Subak irrigation system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subak_\(irrigation\) "Subak \(irrigation\)"), a [UNESCO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-25) It is also home to a unified confederation of kingdoms composed of [10 traditional royal Balinese houses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_current_non-sovereign_Asian_monarchs#Indonesia "List of current non-sovereign Asian monarchs"), each house ruling a specific geographic area. The confederation is the successor of the [Bali Kingdom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_Kingdom "Bali Kingdom"). The royal houses, which originated before [Dutch colonisation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies"), are not recognised by the government of Indonesia.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-26)
## Etymology
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]
The name _Bali_ is attested in ancient Balinese inscriptions as part of the compound *Vāli-dvīpa* (or *Wāli-dvīpa*), where [Sanskrit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") **dvīpa** (Sanskrit: द्वीप) means "island".[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-27)
One of the earliest attestations of *Vāli-dvīpa* occurs in the [Belanjong (Blanjong) pillar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Belanjong_pillar "Belanjong pillar") inscription, dated to [Saka year](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_saka_calendar "Balinese saka calendar") 835 / Phalguna month, under King [Sri Kesari Warmadewa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sri_Kesari_Warmadewa "Sri Kesari Warmadewa") (c. 914 CE).[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-28)
The precise meaning of the element *Vāli* (or *Bali*) in *Vāli-dvīpa* is not certain. Some hypothesized interpretations include: 
  * That *Bali* derives from Sanskrit *bali* (बलि), meaning "offering", "tribute", or "sacrifice", thus rendering *Bali-dvīpa* as "Island of offerings" or "Island of sacrifice".
  * That *Vāli* may relate to terms signifying "power", or "force", or possibly be a phonetic variation (i.e., alternation of *b* and *v*) common in epigraphy, which would suggest meanings such as "Island of strength" or similar.

## History
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]
Main article: [History of Bali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_Bali "History of Bali")
### Ancient
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ancient")]
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Bali_panorama.jpg/250px-Bali_panorama.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Bali_panorama.jpg)The [Subak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subak_\(irrigation\) "Subak \(irrigation\)") irrigation system has existed since the 9th century.
Bali was inhabited around 2000 BC by [Austronesian peoples](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austronesian_peoples "Austronesian peoples") who migrated originally from the island of [Taiwan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") to Southeast Asia and [Oceania](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oceania "Oceania") through [Maritime Southeast Asia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maritime_Southeast_Asia "Maritime Southeast Asia").[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-29)[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Hinzler-30) Culturally and linguistically, the Balinese are closely related to the people of the [Indonesian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") archipelago, [Malaysia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Brunei](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), the [Philippines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), and [Oceania](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oceania "Oceania").[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Hinzler-30) Stone tools dating from this time have been found near the village of Cekik in the island's west.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-31)[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Lonely-32)
In ancient Bali, nine Hindu sects existed: the [Pasupata](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pashupata_Shaivism "Pashupata Shaivism"), [Bhairawa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Siddharthanagar "Siddharthanagar"), Siwa Shidanta, [Vaishnava](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vaishnava "Vaishnava"), [Bodha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bodha "Bodha"), [Brahma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brahma "Brahma"), Resi, Sora, and [Ganapatya](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganapatya "Ganapatya"). Each sect revered a specific deity as its personal Godhead.[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-33)
Inscriptions from 896 and 911 do not mention a king until 914, when Sri Kesarivarma, a contemporary of the Kesari dynasty in the [Kalinga Kingdom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kalingga_kingdom "Kalingga kingdom") of ancient India, is mentioned. They also reveal an independent Bali, with a distinct dialect, being influenced by the Sanskrit and Pali languages, where [Buddhism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Shaivism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shaivism "Shaivism") were practiced simultaneously. [Mpu Sindok](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mpu_Sindok "Mpu Sindok")'s great-granddaughter, [Mahendradatta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahendradatta "Mahendradatta") (Gunapriyadharmapatni), married the Bali king [Udayana Warmadewa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Udayana_Warmadewa "Udayana Warmadewa") (Dharmodayanavarmadeva) around 989, giving birth to [Airlangga](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Airlangga "Airlangga") around 1001. This marriage also brought more [Hinduism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") and Javanese culture to Bali. Princess Sakalendukirana appeared in 1098. Suradhipa reigned from 1115 to 1119, and Jayasakti from 1146 until 1150. Jayapangus appears on inscriptions between 1178 and 1181, while Adikuntiketana and his son Paramesvara appear in 1204.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-indianized-34): 129, 144, 168, 180 
Balinese culture was strongly influenced by Indian, Chinese, and particularly Kalinga [Hindu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") culture, beginning around the 1st century AD.[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-35) The name _Bali dwipa_ ("Bali island") has been discovered from various inscriptions, including the Blanjong pillar inscription written by [Sri Kesari Warmadewa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sri_Kesari_Warmadewa "Sri Kesari Warmadewa") in 914 AD, and mentioning **Walidwipa**. It was during this time that the people developed their complex irrigation system, _[subak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subak_\(irrigation\) "Subak \(irrigation\)"),_ to grow rice in [wet-field cultivation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paddy_field "Paddy field"). Some religious and cultural traditions still practiced today can be traced to this period. 
The Hindu-Buddhist [Majapahit Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Majapahit "Majapahit") (1293–1520 AD) on eastern [Java](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Java "Java") founded a Balinese colony in 1343. The uncle of [Hayam Wuruk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hayam_Wuruk "Hayam Wuruk") is mentioned in the charters of 1384–86. Mass Javanese immigration to Bali occurred in the next century when the Majapahit Empire fell in 1520.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-indianized-34): 234, 240  Bali's government then became an independent collection of Hindu kingdoms, which led to a Balinese national identity and major enhancements in culture, arts, and economy. The nation with various kingdoms became independent for up to 386 years until 1906, when the Dutch subjugated and repulsed the natives for economic control and took it over.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-36)
### Portuguese contacts
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Portuguese contacts")]
The first known [European](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_people "European people") contact with Bali is thought to have been made in 1512, when a Portuguese expedition led by Antonio Abreu and [Francisco Serrão](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Francisco_Serr%C3%A3o "Francisco Serrão") sighted its northern shores. It was the first expedition of a series of bi-annual fleets to the Moluccas, which throughout the 16th century, traveled along the coasts of the Sunda Islands. Bali was also mapped in 1512, in the chart of Francisco Rodrigues, aboard the expedition.[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-37) In 1585, a ship foundered off the [Bukit Peninsula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bukit_Peninsula "Bukit Peninsula") and left a few Portuguese in the service of [Dewa Agung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dewa_Agung "Dewa Agung").[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-38)
### Dutch East Indies
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Dutch East Indies")]
See also: [Dutch East Indies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/1906_Puputan_monument_in_Denpasar.jpg/250px-1906_Puputan_monument_in_Denpasar.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:1906_Puputan_monument_in_Denpasar.jpg)[Puputan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Puputan "Puputan") monument
In 1597, the Dutch merchant-explorer [Cornelis de Houtman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cornelis_de_Houtman "Cornelis de Houtman") arrived at Bali, and the [Dutch East India Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company") was established in 1602. The Dutch government expanded its control across the Indonesian archipelago during the second half of the 19th century. Dutch political and economic control over Bali began in the 1840s on the island's north coast when the Dutch pitted various competing Balinese realms against each other.[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-ctpqur-39) In the late 1890s, struggles between Balinese kingdoms on the island's south were exploited by the Dutch to increase their control. 
In June 1860, the famous Welsh naturalist, [Alfred Russel Wallace](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alfred_Russel_Wallace "Alfred Russel Wallace"), travelled to Bali from [Singapore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singapore_in_the_Straits_Settlements "Singapore in the Straits Settlements"), landing at [Buleleng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buleleng,_Bali "Buleleng, Bali") on the north coast of the island. Wallace's trip to Bali was instrumental in helping him devise his [Wallace Line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wallace_Line "Wallace Line") theory. The Wallace Line is a faunal boundary that runs through the strait between Bali and [Lombok](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lombok "Lombok"). It is a boundary between species. In his travel memoir _[The Malay Archipelago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Malay_Archipelago "The Malay Archipelago"),_ Wallace wrote of his experience in Bali, which includes a strong mention of the [unique Balinese irrigation methods](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subak_\(irrigation\) "Subak \(irrigation\)"): 
> I was astonished and delighted; as my visit to Java was some years later, I had never beheld so beautiful and well-cultivated a district out of Europe. A slightly undulating plain extends from the seacoast about ten or twelve miles (16 or 19 kilometres) inland, where it is bounded by a fine range of wooded and cultivated hills. Houses and villages, marked out by dense clumps of [coconut palms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coconut "Coconut"), [tamarind](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tamarind "Tamarind") and other fruit trees, are dotted about in every direction; while between them extend luxurious rice grounds, watered by an elaborate system of irrigation that would be the pride of the best-cultivated parts of Europe.[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-40)
The Dutch mounted large naval and ground [assaults at the Sanur region](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dutch_intervention_in_Bali_\(1906\) "Dutch intervention in Bali \(1906\)") in 1906 and were met by the thousands of members of the royal family and their followers, who, rather than yield to the superior Dutch force, committed ritual suicide (_[puputan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Puputan "Puputan")_) to avoid the humiliation of surrender.[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-ctpqur-39) Despite Dutch demands for surrender, an estimated 200 Balinese killed themselves rather than surrender.[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-41) In the [Dutch intervention in Bali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dutch_intervention_in_Bali_\(1908\) "Dutch intervention in Bali \(1908\)"), a similar mass suicide occurred in the face of a Dutch assault in [Klungkung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Klungkung_Regency "Klungkung Regency"). Afterward, the Dutch governors exercised administrative control over the island, but local control over religion and culture generally remained intact. Dutch rule over Bali came later and was never as well established as in other parts of Indonesia, such as Java and [Maluku](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maluku_Islands "Maluku Islands"). 
In the 1930s, anthropologists [Margaret Mead](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Margaret_Mead "Margaret Mead") and [Gregory Bateson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gregory_Bateson "Gregory Bateson"), artists [Miguel Covarrubias](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miguel_Covarrubias "Miguel Covarrubias") and [Walter Spies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walter_Spies "Walter Spies"), and musicologist [Colin McPhee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Colin_McPhee "Colin McPhee") all spent time here. Their accounts of the island and its peoples created a Western image of Bali as "an enchanted land of [aesthetes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aestheticism "Aestheticism") at peace with themselves and nature". Soon after, Western tourists began to visit the island.[[40]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Friend-42) The sensuous image of Bali was enhanced in the West by a quasi-pornographic 1932 documentary, _Virgins of Bali_ , about a day in the lives of two teenage Balinese girls, who the film's narrator Deane Dickason notes in the first scene "bathe their shamelessly nude bronze bodies."[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-doherty-43): 134  Under the looser version of the [Hays code](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hays_code "Hays code") that existed up to 1934, nudity involving "civilised" (i.e., white) women was banned, but permitted with "uncivilised" (i.e., all non-white women), a loophole that was exploited by the producers of _Virgins of Bali_.[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-doherty-43): 133  The film, which mostly consisted of scenes of topless Balinese women, was a great success in 1932, and was perhaps the main catalyst for the popularity of Bali among tourists.[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-doherty-43): 135  The Dutch also dedicated significant efforts to implement _Baliseering_ (lit.'[Balinization](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Balinization&action=edit&redlink=1 "Balinization \(page does not exist\)") [[id](https://id.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinisasi "id:Balinisasi")]') politics to maintain traditions on the island, as well as preventing the [Islamization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spread_of_Islam "Spread of Islam") of the Islamic sultanates from [Java](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Java "Java") and banning [Christian missionaries](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christian_mission "Christian mission")' activities. 
[Imperial Japan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") occupied Bali during World War II. It was not originally a target in their Netherlands East Indies Campaign; however, as the airfields on [Borneo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Borneo "Borneo") were inoperative due to heavy rains, the [Imperial Japanese Army](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Army "Imperial Japanese Army") decided to occupy Bali, which did not suffer from comparable weather. The island had no regular [Royal Netherlands East Indies Army](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Royal_Netherlands_East_Indies_Army "Royal Netherlands East Indies Army") (KNIL) troops. There was only a Native Auxiliary Corps _Prajoda_ (Korps Prajoda) consisting of about 600 native soldiers and several Dutch KNIL officers under the command of KNIL Lieutenant Colonel W.P. Roodenburg. On 19 February 1942, the Japanese forces landed near the town of Sanoer (Sanur) and the island was quickly captured.[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-44)
During the Japanese occupation, a Balinese military officer, [I Gusti Ngurah Rai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/I_Gusti_Ngurah_Rai "I Gusti Ngurah Rai"), formed a Balinese 'freedom army'. The harsh treatment of the Balinese by the Japanese occupation forces fomented more resentment than the former Dutch colonial rulers.[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-45)
### Independence from the Dutch
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Independence from the Dutch")]
In 1945, Bali was liberated by the British [5th Infantry Division](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/5th_Infantry_Division_\(India\) "5th Infantry Division \(India\)") under the command of Major-General [Robert Mansergh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Mansergh "Robert Mansergh"), who took the Japanese surrender. Once Japanese forces had been repatriated, the island was handed over to the Dutch the following year.[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-46)
In 1946, the Dutch constituted Bali as one of the 13 administrative districts of the newly proclaimed [State of East Indonesia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/State_of_East_Indonesia "State of East Indonesia"), a rival state to the Republic of Indonesia, which was proclaimed and headed by [Sukarno](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno") and [Hatta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mohammad_Hatta "Mohammad Hatta"). Bali was included in the "[Republic of the United States of Indonesia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Republic_of_the_United_States_of_Indonesia "Republic of the United States of Indonesia")" when the Netherlands recognised Indonesian independence on 29 December 1949.[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-47) The first governor of Bali, [Anak Agung Bagus Suteja](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anak_Agung_Bagus_Suteja "Anak Agung Bagus Suteja"), was appointed by President Sukarno in 1958, when Bali became a province.[[46]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-48)
### Contemporary
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Contemporary")]
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Bali_memorial.jpg/250px-Bali_memorial.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Bali_memorial.jpg)[2002 Bali bombings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2002_Bali_bombings "2002 Bali bombings") memorial
The 1963 eruption of [Mount Agung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Agung "Mount Agung") killed thousands, created economic havoc, and forced many displaced Balinese to be [transmigrated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transmigration_program "Transmigration program") to other parts of Indonesia. Mirroring the widening of social divisions across Indonesia in the 1950s and early 1960s, Bali saw conflict between supporters of the traditional [caste system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Caste "Caste") and those rejecting this system. Politically, the opposition was represented by supporters of the [Indonesian Communist Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Indonesia "Communist Party of Indonesia") (PKI) and the [Indonesian Nationalist Party](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesian_National_Party "Indonesian National Party") (PNI), with tensions and ill-feeling further increased by the PKI's land reform programmes.[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-ctpqur-39) [A purported coup attempt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/30_September_Movement "30 September Movement") in Jakarta was averted by forces led by General Suharto. 
The army became the dominant power as it instigated [a violent anti-communist purge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesian_mass_killings_of_1965%E2%80%9366 "Indonesian mass killings of 1965–66") backed by Western powers, in which the army blamed the PKI for the coup. Most estimates suggest that at least 500,000 people were killed across Indonesia, with an estimated 80,000 killed in Bali, equivalent to 5% of the island's population.[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-ctpqur-39)[[40]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Friend-42)[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Ricklefs-49) With no Islamic forces involved as in Java and Sumatra, upper-caste PNI landlords led the extermination of PKI members.[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Ricklefs-49)
As a result of the 1965–66 massacres, [Suharto](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Suharto "Suharto") was able to manoeuvre Sukarno [out of the presidency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transition_to_the_New_Order "Transition to the New Order"). His ["New Order"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Order_\(Indonesia\) "New Order \(Indonesia\)") government re-established relations with Western countries. The pre-war Bali, as "paradise," was revived in a modern form. The resulting large growth in tourism has led to a dramatic increase in Balinese standards of living and significant foreign exchange earned for the country.[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-ctpqur-39)
[A bombing in 2002](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2002_Bali_bombings "2002 Bali bombings") by militant [Islamists](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamism "Islamism") in the tourist area of [Kuta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kuta,_Bali "Kuta, Bali") killed 202 people, mostly foreigners. This attack, and [another in 2005](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2005_Bali_bombings "2005 Bali bombings"), severely reduced tourism, producing much economic hardship on the island. 
On [9 July 2008](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008_Bali_gubernatorial_election "2008 Bali gubernatorial election"), for the first time in Bali's history, the election for [governor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Governor_of_Bali "Governor of Bali") and [vice governor of Bali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vice_Governor_of_Bali "Vice Governor of Bali") was democratically elected by the people of Bali directly. 
On 27 November 2017, Mount Agung erupted five times, causing the evacuation of thousands, disrupting air travel, and causing much environmental damage. [Further eruptions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2017%E2%80%932019_eruptions_of_Mount_Agung "2017–2019 eruptions of Mount Agung") also occurred between 2018 and 2019.[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-50)
On 15–16 November 2022, with the [2022 G20 Bali summit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2022_G20_Bali_summit "2022 G20 Bali summit"), the [seventeenth meeting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_G20_summits "List of G20 summits") of the [Group of Twenty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/G20 "G20") (G20) was held in [Nusa Dua](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Dua "Nusa Dua").[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-host-51)
## Geography
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geography")]
See also: [List of bodies of water in Bali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_bodies_of_water_in_Bali "List of bodies of water in Bali") and [List of mountains in Bali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_mountains_in_Bali "List of mountains in Bali")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/BaliLandsat001.jpg/250px-BaliLandsat001.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:BaliLandsat001.jpg)Aerial photograph of Bali [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Bali2022OSM.png/250px-Bali2022OSM.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Bali2022OSM.png)Detailed map of Bali
The island of Bali lies 3.2 km (2.0 mi) east of [Java](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Java_\(island\) "Java \(island\)"), and is approximately [8 degrees south](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/8th_parallel_south "8th parallel south") of the [equator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equator "Equator"). Bali and Java are separated by the [Bali Strait](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_Strait "Bali Strait"). East to west, the island is approximately 153 km (95 mi) wide and spans approximately 112 km (70 mi) north to south; administratively it covers 5,590.15 km2 (2,158.37 sq mi), or 5,387.31 km2 (2,080.05 sq mi) excluding offshore [Nusa Penida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Penida "Nusa Penida") District,[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-52) which comprises three small islands off the southeast coast of Bali. Its population density was roughly 798 people/km2 (2,070 people/mi2) in mid 2024. 
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Bali%27s_Gunung_Agung_seen_at_sunset_from_Gunung_Rinjani.jpg/250px-Bali%27s_Gunung_Agung_seen_at_sunset_from_Gunung_Rinjani.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Bali%27s_Gunung_Agung_seen_at_sunset_from_Gunung_Rinjani.jpg)[Mount Agung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Agung "Mount Agung") is the highest point of Bali.
Bali's central mountains include several peaks over 2,000 metres (6,600 feet) in elevation and active volcanoes such as [Mount Batur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Batur "Mount Batur"). The highest is [Mount Agung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Agung "Mount Agung") (3,031 m; 9,944 ft), known as the "mother mountain", which is an active [volcano](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Volcano "Volcano") rated as one of the world's most likely sites for a massive eruption within the next 100 years.[[51]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-53) In late 2017, [Mount Agung started erupting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Agung#2017_seismic_activity_and_eruption "Mount Agung") and large numbers of people were evacuated, temporarily closing the island's airport.[[52]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-54) Mountains range from centre to the eastern side, with Mount Agung the easternmost peak. Bali's volcanic nature has contributed to its exceptional fertility, and its tall mountain ranges provide the high rainfall that supports the highly productive agricultural sector. South of the mountains is a broad, steadily descending area where most of Bali's large rice crop is grown. The northern side of the mountains slopes more steeply to the sea and is the main coffee-producing area of the island, along with rice, vegetables, and cattle. The longest river, [Ayung River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ayung_River "Ayung River"), flows approximately 75 km (47 mi) (see [List of rivers of Bali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_rivers_of_Bali "List of rivers of Bali")). 
The island is surrounded by [coral reefs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coral_reef "Coral reef"). Beaches in the south tend to have white sand, while those in the north and west have [black sand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_sand "Black sand"). Bali has no major waterways, although the Ho River is navigable by small _[sampan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sampan "Sampan")_ boats. Black sand beaches between Pasut and Klatingdukuh are being developed for tourism, but apart from the seaside temple of [Tanah Lot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tanah_Lot "Tanah Lot"), they are not yet used for significant tourism. 
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Wonderfull_Nusa_Penida.jpg/250px-Wonderfull_Nusa_Penida.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Wonderfull_Nusa_Penida.jpg)The cliff of [Nusa Penida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Penida "Nusa Penida") with [Kelingking Beach](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kelingking_Beach&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kelingking Beach \(page does not exist\)") [[id](https://id.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pantai_Kelingking "id:Pantai Kelingking")] in the foreground
The largest city is the provincial capital, [Denpasar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Denpasar "Denpasar"), near the southern coast. Its population is around 755,600 (in mid 2024).[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Badan_Pusat_Statistik_2025-7) Bali's second-largest city is the old colonial capital, [Singaraja](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singaraja "Singaraja"), which is located on the north coast and whose urban area is home to around 150,000 people in 2024.[[53]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-55) Other important cities include the beach resort, [Kuta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kuta,_Bali "Kuta, Bali"), which is practically part of Denpasar's urban area, and [Ubud](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ubud "Ubud"), situated at the north of Denpasar, which is regarded as the island's cultural centre.[[54]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-56)
Three small islands lie to the immediate south-east and all are administratively part of the [Klungkung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Klungkung_Regency "Klungkung Regency") regency of Bali: [Nusa Penida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Penida "Nusa Penida"), [Nusa Lembongan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Lembongan "Nusa Lembongan") and [Nusa Ceningan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Ceningan "Nusa Ceningan"). These islands are separated from Bali by the [Badung Strait](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Badung_Strait "Badung Strait"). 
To the east, the [Lombok Strait](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lombok_Strait "Lombok Strait") separates Bali from [Lombok](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lombok "Lombok") and marks the [biogeographical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biogeography "Biogeography") division between the fauna of the [Indomalayan realm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indomalayan_realm "Indomalayan realm") and the distinctly different fauna of [Australasia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Australasian_realm "Australasian realm"). The transition is known as the [Wallace Line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wallace_Line "Wallace Line"), named after [Alfred Russel Wallace](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alfred_Russel_Wallace "Alfred Russel Wallace"), who first proposed a transition zone between these two major [biomes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biome "Biome"). When sea levels dropped during the [Pleistocene ice age](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quaternary_glaciation "Quaternary glaciation"), Bali was connected to Java and [Sumatra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra") and to the mainland of Asia and shared the Asian fauna, but the deep water of the Lombok Strait continued to keep Lombok Island and the [Lesser Sunda archipelago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lesser_Sunda_Islands "Lesser Sunda Islands") isolated. 
### Climate
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]
Being just 8 degrees south of the equator, Bali has a fairly even climate all year round. Average year-round temperature stands at around 30 °C (86 °F) with a humidity level of about 85%.[[55]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-57)
Daytime temperatures at low elevations vary between 20 and 33 °C (68 and 91 °F), but the temperatures decrease significantly with increasing elevation. 
The west monsoon is in place from approximately October to April, and this can bring significant rain, particularly from December to March. During the rainy season, there are comparatively fewer tourists seen in Bali.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_] During the Easter and Christmas holidays, the weather is very unpredictable. Outside of the monsoon period, humidity is relatively low and any rain is unlikely in lowland areas.

## Flora and fauna
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Flora and fauna")]
See also: [Conservation in Indonesia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conservation_in_Indonesia "Conservation in Indonesia")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Bali_Starling_-_Bali_Barat_MG_8974_%2829654702785%29.jpg/250px-Bali_Starling_-_Bali_Barat_MG_8974_%2829654702785%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Bali_Starling_-_Bali_Barat_MG_8974_\(29654702785\).jpg)[Bali myna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_myna "Bali myna") (Jalak Bali) is one of the protected birds located in [West Bali National Park](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Bali_National_Park "West Bali National Park") [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Bali_%E2%80%93_Uluwatu_Sunset_Temple_%282688001517%29.jpg/250px-Bali_%E2%80%93_Uluwatu_Sunset_Temple_%282688001517%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Bali_%E2%80%93_Uluwatu_Sunset_Temple_\(2688001517\).jpg)[Crab-eating macaque monkeys](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crab-eating_macaque "Crab-eating macaque") in [Uluwatu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uluwatu "Uluwatu")
Bali lies just to the west of the [Wallace Line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wallace_Line "Wallace Line"),[[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-58) and thus has a fauna that is Asian in character, with very little Australasian influence, and has more in common with Java than with Lombok.[[57]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-59) An exception is the [yellow-crested cockatoo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yellow-crested_cockatoo "Yellow-crested cockatoo"), a member of a primarily Australasian family. There are around 280 species of birds, including the critically endangered [Bali myna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_myna "Bali myna"), which is [endemic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Endemism "Endemism"). Others include [barn swallow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barn_swallow "Barn swallow"), [black-naped oriole](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black-naped_oriole "Black-naped oriole"), [black racket-tailed treepie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_racket-tailed_treepie "Black racket-tailed treepie"), [crested serpent-eagle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crested_serpent-eagle "Crested serpent-eagle"), [crested treeswift](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crested_treeswift "Crested treeswift"), [dollarbird](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dollarbird "Dollarbird"), [Java sparrow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Java_sparrow "Java sparrow"), [lesser adjutant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lesser_adjutant "Lesser adjutant"), [long-tailed shrike](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Long-tailed_shrike "Long-tailed shrike"), [milky stork](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milky_stork "Milky stork"), [Pacific swallow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_swallow "Pacific swallow"), [red-rumped swallow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red-rumped_swallow "Red-rumped swallow"), [sacred kingfisher](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sacred_kingfisher "Sacred kingfisher"), [sea eagle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sea_eagle_\(bird\) "Sea eagle \(bird\)"), [woodswallow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Woodswallow "Woodswallow"), [savanna nightjar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Savanna_nightjar "Savanna nightjar"), [stork-billed kingfisher](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stork-billed_kingfisher "Stork-billed kingfisher"), [yellow-vented bulbul](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yellow-vented_bulbul "Yellow-vented bulbul") and [great egret](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_egret "Great egret"). 
Until the early 20th century, Bali was possibly home to several [large mammals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Megafauna "Megafauna"): [banteng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banteng "Banteng"), [leopard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leopard "Leopard") and the endemic [Bali tiger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_tiger "Bali tiger"). The banteng still occurs in its domestic form, whereas leopards are found only in neighbouring Java, and the Bali tiger is extinct. The last definite record of a tiger on Bali dates from 1937 when one was shot, though the subspecies may have survived until the 1940s or 1950s.[[58]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-P.tigris-60) Pleistocene and Holocene megafaunas include [banteng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banteng "Banteng") and [giant tapir](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Giant_tapir "Giant tapir") (based on speculations that they might have reached up to the [Wallace Line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wallace_Line "Wallace Line")),[[59]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-chazine-61) and [rhinoceros](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rhinoceros "Rhinoceros").[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-62)
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Monkey_Forest.jpg/250px-Monkey_Forest.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Monkey_Forest.jpg)[Ubud Monkey Forest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ubud_Monkey_Forest "Ubud Monkey Forest")
[Squirrels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Squirrel "Squirrel") are quite commonly encountered, less often is the [Asian palm civet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asian_palm_civet "Asian palm civet"), which is also kept in coffee farms to produce [kopi luwak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kopi_luwak "Kopi luwak"). [Bats](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bat "Bat") are well represented, perhaps the most famous place to encounter them remaining is the Goa Lawah (Temple of the Bats) where they are worshipped by the locals and also constitute a tourist attraction. They also occur in other cave temples, for instance at Gangga Beach. Two species of [monkey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Old_World_monkey "Old World monkey") occur. The [crab-eating macaque](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crab-eating_macaque "Crab-eating macaque"), known locally as "kera", is quite common around human settlements and temples, where it becomes accustomed to being fed by humans, particularly in any of the three "monkey forest" temples, such as the popular one in the [Ubud](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ubud "Ubud") area. They are also quite often kept as pets by locals. The second monkey, endemic to Java and some surrounding islands such as Bali, is far rarer and more elusive and is the [Javan langur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Javan_langur "Javan langur"), locally known as "lutung". They occur in a few places apart from the [West Bali National Park](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Bali_National_Park "West Bali National Park"). They are born an orange colour, though they would have already changed to a more blackish colouration by their first year.[[61]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-63) In Java, however, there is more of a tendency for this species to retain its juvenile orange colour into adulthood, and a mixture of black and orange monkeys can be seen together as a family. Other rarer mammals include the [Sunda leopard cat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunda_leopard_cat "Sunda leopard cat"), [Sunda pangolin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunda_pangolin "Sunda pangolin") and [black giant squirrel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_giant_squirrel "Black giant squirrel"). Snakes include the [king cobra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/King_cobra "King cobra") and [reticulated python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reticulated_python "Reticulated python"). The [water monitor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asian_water_monitor "Asian water monitor") can grow to at least 1.5 m (4.9 ft) in length and 50 kg (110 lb)[[62]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-64) and can move quickly. 
The rich coral reefs around the coast, particularly around popular diving spots such as [Tulamben](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tulamben "Tulamben"), [Amed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amed_\(Bali\) "Amed \(Bali\)"), [Menjangan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Menjangan_Island "Menjangan Island") or neighbouring [Nusa Penida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Penida "Nusa Penida"), host a wide range of marine life, for instance [hawksbill turtle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hawksbill_turtle "Hawksbill turtle"), [giant sunfish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mola_mola "Mola mola"), [giant manta ray](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manta_ray "Manta ray"), [giant moray](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Giant_moray "Giant moray") eel, [bumphead parrotfish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bumphead_parrotfish "Bumphead parrotfish"), [hammerhead shark](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hammerhead_shark "Hammerhead shark"), [reef shark](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grey_reef_shark "Grey reef shark"), [barracuda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barracuda "Barracuda"), and [sea snakes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sea_snake "Sea snake"). Dolphins are commonly encountered on the north coast near [Singaraja](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singaraja "Singaraja") and [Lovina](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lovina "Lovina").[[63]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-65)
A team of scientists surveyed from 29 April 2011, to 11 May 2011, at 33 sea sites around Bali. They discovered 952 species of reef fish of which 8 were new discoveries at Pemuteran, Gilimanuk, [Nusa Dua](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Dua "Nusa Dua"), [Tulamben](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tulamben "Tulamben") and [Candidasa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Candidasa "Candidasa"), and 393 coral species, including two new ones at [Padangbai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Padangbai "Padangbai") and between Padangbai and [Amed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amed_\(Bali\) "Amed \(Bali\)").[[64]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-66) The average coverage level of healthy coral was 36% (better than in [Raja Ampat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raja_Ampat "Raja Ampat") and [Halmahera](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Halmahera "Halmahera") by 29% or in [Fakfak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fakfak "Fakfak") and [Kaimana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kaimana "Kaimana") by 25%) with the highest coverage found in Gili Selang and Gili Mimpang in [Candidasa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Candidasa "Candidasa"), [Karangasem Regency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karangasem_Regency "Karangasem Regency").[[65]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-67) Among the larger trees the most common are: [banyan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banyan "Banyan") trees, [jackfruit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jackfruit "Jackfruit"), [coconuts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coconut "Coconut"), [bamboo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bamboo "Bamboo") species, [acacia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acacia "Acacia") trees and also endless rows of coconuts and banana species. Numerous flowers can be seen: [hibiscus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hibiscus "Hibiscus"), [frangipani](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frangipani "Frangipani"), [bougainvillea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bougainvillea "Bougainvillea"), [poinsettia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poinsettia "Poinsettia"), [oleander](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oleander "Oleander"), [jasmine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jasmine "Jasmine"), [water lily](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nymphaeaceae "Nymphaeaceae"), [lotus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nelumbo "Nelumbo"), [roses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rose "Rose"), [begonias](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Begonia "Begonia"), orchids and [hydrangeas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrangea "Hydrangea") exist. On higher grounds that receive more moisture, for instance, around [Kintamani](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kintamani,_Bali "Kintamani, Bali"), certain species of [fern](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fern "Fern") trees, [mushrooms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mushroom "Mushroom") and even [pine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pine "Pine") trees thrive well. Rice comes in many varieties. Other plants with agricultural value include: [salak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salak "Salak"), [mangosteen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mangosteen "Mangosteen"), [corn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maize "Maize"), Kintamani orange, coffee and [water spinach](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_spinach "Water spinach").[[66]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-68)
## Environment
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Environment")]
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Uluwatu%40bali.jpg/250px-Uluwatu%40bali.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Uluwatu@bali.jpg)[Uluwatu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uluwatu "Uluwatu") [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/DJI_0128_Bali_Botanic_Garden.jpg/250px-DJI_0128_Bali_Botanic_Garden.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:DJI_0128_Bali_Botanic_Garden.jpg)[Bali Botanic Garden](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_Botanic_Garden "Bali Botanic Garden") in [Bedugul](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bedugul "Bedugul")
Over-exploitation by the [tourist industry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tourism_in_Indonesia "Tourism in Indonesia") has led to 200 out of 400 rivers on the island drying up. Research suggests that the southern part of Bali would face a water shortage.[[67]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-69) To ease the shortage, the central government plans to build a water catchment and processing facility at Petanu River in Gianyar. The 300 litres capacity of water per second will be channelled to Denpasar, Badung and Gianyar in 2013.[[68]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-70)
A 2010 Environment Ministry report on its environmental quality index gave Bali a score of 99.65, which was the highest score of Indonesia's 33 provinces. The score considers the level of [total suspended solids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_suspended_solids "Total suspended solids"), [dissolved oxygen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dissolved_oxygen "Dissolved oxygen"), and [chemical oxygen demand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical_oxygen_demand "Chemical oxygen demand") in water.[[69]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-71)
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Taman_Nasional_Bali_Barat%2C_2019.jpg/250px-Taman_Nasional_Bali_Barat%2C_2019.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Taman_Nasional_Bali_Barat,_2019.jpg)[West Bali National Park](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Bali_National_Park "West Bali National Park")
Erosion at Lebih Beach has seen seven metres (23 feet) of land lost every year. Decades ago, this beach was used for holy pilgrimages with more than 10,000 people, but they have now moved to [Masceti Beach](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Masceti_Beach "Masceti Beach").[[70]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-72)
In 2017, a year when Bali received nearly 5.7 million tourists, government officials declared a "garbage emergency" in response to the covering of 3.6-mile stretch of coastline in plastic waste brought in by the tide, amid concerns that the pollution could dissuade visitors from returning.[[71]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-telegraph_plastic-73) Indonesia is one of the world's worst plastic polluters, with some estimates suggesting the country is the source of around 10 per cent of the world's plastic waste. 
On 3 February 2026, Bali launched a major beach clean up after President Prabowo Subianto's criticisms of the Bali's growing waste problem. Hundreds of personnel from the Bali Police and the local military joined students and volunteers to collect rubbish from two of the island’s most popular tourist beaches: Kuta and Kedonganan in Badung Regency.[[72]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-74)
## Government and politics
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Government and politics")]
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Kantor_Gubernur_Bali_%2830857458522%29.jpg/960px-Kantor_Gubernur_Bali_%2830857458522%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Kantor_Gubernur_Bali_\(30857458522\).jpg)
Bali Governor's Office
The [governor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Governor_of_Bali "Governor of Bali") is the highest officeholder in the Bali provincial government with the [vice governor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vice_Governor_of_Bali "Vice Governor of Bali") being the second highest officeholder. The Governor of Bali is responsible for the territory of the Province of Bali. The current governor in office in the province of Bali is [I Wayan Koster](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/I_Wayan_Koster "I Wayan Koster") who was first being elected in the [2018 Bali gubernatorial election](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2018_Bali_gubernatorial_election "2018 Bali gubernatorial election") accompanied by his first running mate at that time [Tjokorda Oka Artha Ardana Sukawati](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tjokorda_Oka_Artha_Ardana_Sukawati "Tjokorda Oka Artha Ardana Sukawati") (Cok Ace). Koster is the 8th governor of Bali, since the province was formed into the province of Bali in 1958 based on Law Number 64 of 1958. Koster and Cok Ace were inaugurated by the [president](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/President_of_Indonesia "President of Indonesia") of the [Republic of Indonesia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Republic_of_Indonesia "Republic of Indonesia") at that time [Joko Widodo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joko_Widodo "Joko Widodo"), at the [State Palace](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Istana_Negara_\(Jakarta\) "Istana Negara \(Jakarta\)"), [Jakarta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") on September 5, 2018, for the five years term of office (2018–2023).[[73]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-GOVERNOR-75) After his term of office ended, [Sang Made Mahendra Jaya](https://id.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sang_Made_Mahendra_Jaya "id:Sang Made Mahendra Jaya") was inaugurated as acting governor of Bali on September 5, 2023 with a limited authority in the government. After re-elected in [2024](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2024_Bali_gubernatorial_election "2024 Bali gubernatorial election") [I Wayan Koster](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/I_Wayan_Koster "I Wayan Koster") and [I Nyoman Giri Prasta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/I_Nyoman_Giri_Prasta "I Nyoman Giri Prasta") was inaugurated on February 20, 2025 at the State Palace by the incumbent President of Indonesia [Prabowo Subianto](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prabowo_Subianto "Prabowo Subianto") for the five years term of office (2025–2030).
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Gedung_Utama_DPRD_Provinsi_Bali_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Gedung_Utama_DPRD_Provinsi_Bali_-_panoramio.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Gedung_Utama_DPRD_Provinsi_Bali_-_panoramio.jpg)Bali's [parliament](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_Regional_House_of_Representatives "Bali Regional House of Representatives") (DPRD) building
In the [national legislature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/People%27s_Representative_Council "People's Representative Council"), Bali is represented by nine members,[[74]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-borong-76) with a single electoral district covering the whole province.[[75]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-77) The [Bali Regional House of Representatives](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_Regional_House_of_Representatives "Bali Regional House of Representatives") (DPRD), the provincial legislature, has 55 members.[[76]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-78) The province's politics has historically been dominated by the [Indonesian Democratic Party of Struggle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesian_Democratic_Party_of_Struggle "Indonesian Democratic Party of Struggle") (PDI-P), which has won by far the most votes in every election in Bali since the [first free elections in 1999](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1999_Indonesian_legislative_election "1999 Indonesian legislative election"), it currently forms the ruling government in the province.[[77]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-79)
### Administrative divisions
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]
Main articles: [List of regencies and cities in Bali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_regencies_and_cities_in_Bali "List of regencies and cities in Bali") and [List of districts of Bali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_districts_of_Bali "List of districts of Bali")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Peta_Kecamatan_Provinsi_Bali.png/250px-Peta_Kecamatan_Provinsi_Bali.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Peta_Kecamatan_Provinsi_Bali.png)Map of [districts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Districts_of_Indonesia "Districts of Indonesia") (_kecamatan_) of Bali
The province is divided into eight [regencies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regencies_of_Indonesia "Regencies of Indonesia") (_kabupaten_) and one [city](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cities_of_Indonesia "Cities of Indonesia") (_kota_) together divided into 57 [districts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kecamatan "Kecamatan") (_kecamatan_), in turn sub-divided into 80 [urban villages](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kelurahan "Kelurahan") (_kelurahan_) and 636 [rural villages](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desa_\(Indonesia\) "Desa \(Indonesia\)") (_desa_); all the regencies were originally inaugurated on 9 August 1958,[[78]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-80) while the city of Denpasar were created from part of Badung Regency on 15 January 1992.
