The province forms one of Indonesia's 84 national [electoral districts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electoral_district "Electoral district") to elect members to the Indonesian parliament. The **Bali Electoral District** consists of all of the 8 regencies in the province, together with the city of Denpasar, and elects 9 members to the People's Representative Council.[[82]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-84)
## Economy
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Economy")]
In the 1970s, the Balinese economy was largely agriculture-based in terms of both output and employment.[[83]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-85) Tourism is now the largest single industry in terms of income, and as a result, Bali is one of Indonesia's wealthiest regions. In 2003, around 80% of Bali's economy was tourism related.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-tourism-17) By the end of June 2011, the rate of [non-performing loans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-performing_loan "Non-performing loan") of all banks in Bali were 2.23%, lower than the average of Indonesian banking industry non-performing loan rates (about 5%).[[84]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-86) The economy, however, suffered significantly as a result of the terrorist bombings in [2002](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2002_Bali_bombing "2002 Bali bombing") and [2005](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2005_Bali_bombing "2005 Bali bombing"). The tourism industry has since recovered from these events. 
### Agriculture
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Agriculture")]
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Wood_carving_in_Bali.jpg/250px-Wood_carving_in_Bali.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Wood_carving_in_Bali.jpg)[Wood carving](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wood_carving "Wood carving")
Although tourism produces the GDP's largest output, agriculture is still the island's biggest employer.[[85]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-87) Fishing also provides a significant number of jobs. Bali is also famous for its [artisans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artisan "Artisan") who produce a vast array of handicrafts, including [batik](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Batik "Batik") and [ikat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ikat "Ikat") [cloth and clothing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_Nusra_Tangi "Bali Nusra Tangi"), [wooden carvings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Woodworking "Woodworking"), stone carvings, painted art and silverware. Notably, individual villages typically adopt a single product, such as wind chimes or wooden furniture. 
The [Arabica coffee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coffea_arabica "Coffea arabica") production region is the highland region of [Kintamani](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kintamani,_Bali "Kintamani, Bali") near [Mount Batur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Batur "Mount Batur"). Generally, Balinese coffee is processed using the wet method. This results in a sweet, soft coffee with good consistency. Typical flavours include lemon and other citrus notes.[[86]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-SCAI-88) Many coffee farmers in Kintamani are members of a traditional farming system called [Subak Abian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subak_\(irrigation\) "Subak \(irrigation\)"), which is based on the [Hindu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") philosophy of "[Tri Hita Karana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tri_Hita_Karana "Tri Hita Karana")". According to this philosophy, the three causes of happiness are good relations with [God](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/God "God"), other people, and the environment. The Subak Abian system is ideally suited to the production of [fair trade](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fair_trade_coffee "Fair trade coffee") and [organic coffee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Organic_coffee "Organic coffee") production. Arabica coffee from Kintamani is the first product in Indonesia to request a [geographical indication](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geographical_indication "Geographical indication").[[87]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-BaliKintamani-89)

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Kuta_Beach_%286924448550%29.jpg/250px-Kuta_Beach_%286924448550%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Kuta_Beach_\(6924448550\).jpg)[Kuta Beach](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kuta_Beach "Kuta Beach") is a popular tourist spot. [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Nyepifest_auf_Bali.jpg/250px-Nyepifest_auf_Bali.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Nyepifest_auf_Bali.jpg)_[Ogoh-ogoh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ogoh-ogoh "Ogoh-ogoh")_ procession on the eve of _[Nyepi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nyepi "Nyepi")_ [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Dolphins_and_Sunrise_in_Lovina%2C_Indonesia_Bali_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Dolphins_and_Sunrise_in_Lovina%2C_Indonesia_Bali_-_panoramio.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Dolphins_and_Sunrise_in_Lovina,_Indonesia_Bali_-_panoramio.jpg)[Dolphins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dolphins "Dolphins") and sunrise in [Lovina](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lovina "Lovina")
In 1963 the Bali Beach Hotel in [Sanur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sanur,_Bali "Sanur, Bali") was built by [Sukarno](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno") and boosted tourism in Bali. Before the Bali Beach Hotel construction, there were only three significant tourist-class hotels on the island.[[89]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Vickers89-91) Construction of hotels and restaurants began to spread throughout Bali. Tourism further increased in Bali after the [Ngurah Rai International Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ngurah_Rai_International_Airport "Ngurah Rai International Airport") opened in 1970. The Buleleng regency government encouraged the tourism sector as one of the mainstays for economic progress and social welfare. 
The [tourism industry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tourism_industry "Tourism industry") is primarily focused in the south, while also significant in the other parts of the island. The prominent tourist locations are the town of [Kuta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kuta,_Bali "Kuta, Bali") (with its beach), and its outer suburbs of [Legian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legian "Legian") and [Seminyak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seminyak "Seminyak") (which were once independent townships), the east coast town of [Sanur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sanur_\(Bali\) "Sanur \(Bali\)") (once the only tourist hub), [Ubud](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ubud "Ubud") towards the centre of the island, to the south of the [Ngurah Rai International Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ngurah_Rai_International_Airport "Ngurah Rai International Airport"), [Jimbaran](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jimbaran "Jimbaran") and the newer developments of [Nusa Dua](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Dua "Nusa Dua") and [Pecatu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pecatu "Pecatu"). 
The United States government lifted its travel warnings in 2008. The Australian government issued an advisory on Friday, 4 May 2012, with the overall level of this advisory lowered to 'Exercise a high degree of caution'. The Swedish government issued a new warning on Sunday, 10 June 2012, because of one tourist who died from methanol poisoning.[[90]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-92)[_[failed verification](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")_] Australia last issued an advisory on Monday, 5 January 2015, due to new terrorist threats.[[91]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-93)
An offshoot of tourism is the growing real estate industry. Bali's real estate has been rapidly developing in the main tourist areas of Kuta, Legian, Seminyak, and Oberoi. Most recently, high-end 5-star projects are under development on the Bukit peninsula, on the island's south side. Expensive villas are being developed along the cliff sides of south Bali, with commanding panoramic ocean views. Foreign and domestic, many [Jakarta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") individuals and companies are fairly active, and investment into other areas of the island also continues to grow. Land prices, despite the worldwide economic crisis, have remained stable. 
In the last half of 2008, Indonesia's currency had dropped approximately 30% against the US dollar, providing many overseas visitors with improved value for their currencies. 
Bali's tourism economy survived the terrorist bombings of 2002 and 2005, and the tourism industry has slowly recovered and surpassed its pre-terrorist bombing levels; the long-term trend has been a steady increase in visitor arrivals. In 2010, Bali received 2.57 million foreign tourists, which surpassed the target of 2.0–2.3 million tourists. The average occupancy of starred hotels achieved 65%, so the island still should be able to accommodate tourists for some years without any addition of new rooms/hotels,[[92]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-94) although at the peak season some of them are fully booked. 
Bali received the Best Island award from [Travel and Leisure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Travel_and_Leisure "Travel and Leisure") in 2010.[[93]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-95) Bali won because of its attractive surroundings (both mountain and coastal areas), diverse tourist attractions, excellent international and local restaurants, and the friendliness of the local people. The Balinese culture and its religion are also considered the main factor of the award. One of the most prestigious events that symbolize a strong relationship between a god and its followers is [Kecak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kecak "Kecak") dance. According to BBC Travel released in 2011, Bali is one of the World's Best Islands, ranking second after [Santorini](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santorini "Santorini"), Greece.[[94]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-96)
In 2006, [Elizabeth Gilbert](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elizabeth_Gilbert "Elizabeth Gilbert")'s memoir _[Eat, Pray, Love](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eat_Pray_Love_\(book\) "Eat Pray Love \(book\)")_ was published, and in August 2010 it was adapted into the film _[Eat Pray Love](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eat_Pray_Love "Eat Pray Love")_. It took place at Ubud and [Padang-Padang Beach](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Padang_Padang_Beach "Padang Padang Beach") in Bali. Both the book and the film fuelled a boom in tourism in Ubud, the hill town and cultural and tourist centre that was the focus of Gilbert's quest for balance and love through traditional spirituality and healing.[[95]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-97)
In January 2016, after musician [David Bowie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie") died, it was revealed that in his [will](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Will_and_testament "Will and testament"), Bowie asked for his ashes to be scattered in Bali, conforming to [Buddhist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") rituals. He had visited and performed in several [Southeast Asian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Southeast_Asian "Southeast Asian") cities early in his career, including [Bangkok](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok") and [Singapore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singapore "Singapore").[[96]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-98)
Since 2011, China has displaced Japan as the second-largest supplier of tourists to Bali, while Australia still tops the list while India has also emerged as a greater supplier of tourists. Chinese tourists increased by 17% in 2011 from 2010 due to the impact of [ACFTA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ASEAN%E2%80%93China_Free_Trade_Area "ASEAN–China Free Trade Area") and new direct flights to Bali.[[97]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-99) In January 2012, Chinese tourists increased by 222.18% compared to January 2011, while Japanese tourists declined by 23.54% year on year.[[98]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-100) Bali is a very popular tourist destination for Indians.[[99]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-101)
Bali authorities reported the island had 2.88 million foreign tourists and 5 million domestic tourists in 2012, marginally surpassing the expectations of 2.8 million foreign tourists.[[100]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-balitourism-102)
Based on a [Bank Indonesia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bank_Indonesia "Bank Indonesia") survey in May 2013, 34.39 per cent of tourists are upper-middle class, spending between $1,286 and $5,592, and are dominated by Australia, India, France, China, Germany and the UK. Some Chinese tourists have increased their levels of spending from previous years. 30.26 per cent of tourists are middle class, spending between $662 and $1,285.[[101]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-103) In 2017 it was expected that [Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people") tourists would outnumber [Australian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Australian_people "Australian people") tourists. 
In January 2020, 10,000 Chinese tourists cancelled trips to Bali due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[102]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-104) Because of the COVID-19 pandemic travel restrictions, Bali welcomed 1.07 million international travelers in 2020, most of them between January and March, which is −87% compared to 2019. In the first half of 2021, they welcomed 43 international travelers.[[103]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-105) The pandemic presented a major blow on Bali's tourism-dependent economy. On 3 February 2022, Bali reopened again for the first foreign tourists after 2 years of being closed due to the pandemic.[[104]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-106)
In 2022 Indonesia's Minister of Health, Budi Sadikin, stated that the tourism industry in Bali will be complemented by the [medical industry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medical_tourism "Medical tourism").[[105]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-107)
At the beginning of 2023, the governor of Bali demanded a ban on the use of motorcycles by tourists. This happened after a series of accidents. Wayan Koster proposed to cancel the violators' visas. The move sparked widespread outrage on social media.[[106]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-108)
## Transportation
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Transportation")]
See also: [Bali MRT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_MRT "Bali MRT")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Ngurah_Rai_Internasional_Airport_Welcome_Sign.jpg/250px-Ngurah_Rai_Internasional_Airport_Welcome_Sign.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Ngurah_Rai_Internasional_Airport_Welcome_Sign.jpg)[I Gusti Ngurah Rai International Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ngurah_Rai_International_Airport "Ngurah Rai International Airport")
The [Ngurah Rai International Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ngurah_Rai_Airport "Ngurah Rai Airport") is located near Jimbaran, on the [isthmus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isthmus "Isthmus") at the southernmost part of the island. [Lt. Col. Wisnu Airfield](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lt._Col._Wisnu_Airfield "Lt. Col. Wisnu Airfield") is in northwest Bali. 
A coastal road circles the island, and three major two-lane arteries cross the central mountains at passes reaching 1,750 m in height (at Penelokan). The Ngurah Rai Bypass is a four-lane expressway that partly encircles Denpasar. Bali has no railway lines. There is a car ferry between Gilimanuk on the west coast of Bali to Ketapang on [Java](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Java "Java"). 
In December 2010 the Government of Indonesia invited investors to build a new Tanah Ampo Cruise Terminal at [Karangasem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karangasem_Regency "Karangasem Regency"), Bali with a projected worth of $30 million.[[107]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-109) On 17 July 2011, the first cruise ship ([Sun Princess](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_World "Pacific World")) anchored about 400 metres (1,300 feet) away from the wharf of Tanah Ampo harbour. The current pier is only 154 metres (505 feet) but will eventually be extended to 300 to 350 metres (980–1,150 feet) to accommodate international cruise ships. The harbour is safer than the existing facility at Benoa and has a scenic backdrop of east Bali mountains and green rice fields.[[108]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-110) The tender for improvement was subject to delays, and as of July 2013 the situation was unclear with cruise line operators complaining and even refusing to use the existing facility at Tanah Ampo.[[109]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-111)
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Trans_Sarbagita_bus.JPG/250px-Trans_Sarbagita_bus.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Trans_Sarbagita_bus.JPG)[Trans Sarbagita](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trans_Sarbagita "Trans Sarbagita") bus
A memorandum of understanding was signed by two ministers, Bali's governor and [Indonesian Train Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PT_Kereta_Api "PT Kereta Api") to build 565 kilometres (351 miles) of railway along the coast around the island. As of July 2015, no details of these proposed railways have been released.[[110]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-112)[[111]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-113) In 2019 it was reported in _Gapura Bali_ that [Wayan Koster](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wayan_Koster "Wayan Koster"), governor of Bali, "is keen to improve Bali's transportation infrastructure and is considering plans to build an electric rail network across the island".[[112]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-114)
On 16 March 2011 (Tanjung) Benoa port received the "Best Port Welcome 2010" award from London's "Dream World Cruise Destination" magazine.[[113]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-115) Government plans to expand the role of Benoa port as export-import port to boost Bali's trade and industry sector.[[114]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-116) In 2013, The Tourism and Creative Economy Ministry advised that 306 cruise liners were scheduled to visit Indonesia, an increase of 43 per cent compared to the previous year.[[115]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-117)
In May 2011, an integrated Area Traffic Control System (ATCS) was implemented to reduce traffic jams at four crossing points: Ngurah Rai statue, Dewa Ruci Kuta crossing, Jimbaran crossing and Sanur crossing. ATCS is an integrated system connecting all [traffic lights](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Traffic_lights "Traffic lights"), CCTVs and other traffic signals with a monitoring office at the police headquarters. It has successfully been implemented in other [ASEAN](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN") countries and will be implemented at other crossings in Bali.[[116]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-118)[[117]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-119)
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Tol_Benoa-Jimbaran_%2827432911116%29.jpg/250px-Tol_Benoa-Jimbaran_%2827432911116%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Tol_Benoa-Jimbaran_\(27432911116\).jpg) [Bali Mandara Toll Road](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_Mandara_Toll_Road "Bali Mandara Toll Road") view above the sea
On 21 December 2011, construction started on the Nusa Dua-Benoa-[Ngurah Rai International Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ngurah_Rai_International_Airport "Ngurah Rai International Airport") toll road, which will also provide a special lane for motorcycles. This has been done by seven state-owned enterprises led by PT Jasa Marga with 60% of the shares. PT Jasa Marga Bali Tol will construct the 9.91-kilometre-long (6.16-mile) toll road (totally 12.7 kilometres (7.89 miles) with access road). The construction is estimated to cost Rp.2.49 trillion ($273.9 million). The project goes through 2 kilometres (1 mile) of mangrove forest and through 2.3 kilometres (1.4 miles) of beach, both within 5.4 hectares (13 acres) area. The elevated toll road is built over the mangrove forest on 18,000 concrete pillars that occupied two hectares of mangrove forest. This was compensated by the planting of 300,000 mangrove trees along the road. On 21 December 2011, the Dewa Ruci 450-metre (1,480-foot) underpass has also started on the busy Dewa Ruci junction near Bali Kuta Galeria with an estimated cost of Rp136 billion ($14.9 million) from the state budget.[[118]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-120)[[119]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-121)[[120]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-122) On 23 September 2013, the [Bali Mandara Toll Road](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_Mandara_Toll_Road "Bali Mandara Toll Road") was opened, with the Dewa Ruci Junction underpass being opened previously.[[121]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-123)
To solve chronic traffic problems, the province will also build a toll road connecting Serangan with Tohpati, a toll road connecting Kuta, Denpasar, and Tohpati, and a flyover connecting Kuta and Ngurah Rai Airport.[[122]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-124)

The population of Bali was 3,890,757 according to the 2010 census, and 4,317,404 at the 2020 census; the official estimate as at mid 2024 was 4,461,260 (comprising 2,222,440 males and 2,210,820 females).[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Badan_Pusat_Statistik_2025-7) In 2021, the Indonesian Ministry of Tourism estimated that there were 109,801 foreigners living on Bali, with most originating from Russia, United States, Australia, United Kingdom, Germany, Japan, France, Italy, India, and the Netherlands.[[124]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-126)
### Ethnic groups
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Ethnic groups")]
#### Ethnic origins
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Ethnic origins")]
A DNA study in 2005 by Karafet et al., found that 84% of Balinese [Y-chromosomes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y-chromosome "Y-chromosome") are of likely [Austronesian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austronesian_peoples "Austronesian peoples") origin, 12% of likely Indian origin and 2% of likely [Melanesian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melanesians "Melanesians") origin.[[125]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-127) According to a recent genetic study, the Balinese, together with the [Javanese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Javanese_people "Javanese people") and [Sundanese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sundanese_people "Sundanese people"), have a significant admixture of [Austroasiatic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austroasiatic "Austroasiatic") and [Austronesian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austronesian_peoples "Austronesian peoples") ancestries.[[126]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-128)
#### Ethnicity
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Ethnicity")]
The majority of the population inhabiting the province of Bali are the local ethnic, namely the [Balinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_people "Balinese people") and indigenous [Bali Aga](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_Aga "Bali Aga") people. The Balinese have a cultural richness that is known worldwide, so that Bali is the main destination for foreign tourists to [Indonesia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"). In addition to the rich beaches, the culture that is of interest in Bali is its dances, such as the [Kecak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kecak_dance "Kecak dance") dance, festivals such as [Ogoh-ogoh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ogoh-ogoh "Ogoh-ogoh"), and others. The largest ethnic group outside the Balinese people is the [Javanese people](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Javanese_people "Javanese people"). 
Based on data from the [2010 Indonesian population census](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2010_Indonesian_census "2010 Indonesian census"), the following is the composition of ethnic groups in the province of Bali:[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-SUKU-8)

### Caste system
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Caste system")]
Main article: [Balinese caste system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_caste_system "Balinese caste system")
Pre-modern Bali had four castes, as Jeff Lewis and Belinda Lewis state, but with a "very strong tradition of communal decision-making and interdependence".[[127]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-129) The four castes have been classified as Sudra ([Shudra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shudra "Shudra")), Wesia ([Vaishyas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vaishya "Vaishya")), [Satria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_Kshatriya "Balinese Kshatriya") ([Kshatriyas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kshatriya "Kshatriya")) and Brahmana ([Brahmin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin")).[[128]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-130)
The 19th-century scholars such as Crawfurd and Friederich suggested that the Balinese caste system had Indian origins, but Helen Creese states that scholars such as Brumund who had visited and stayed on the island of Bali suggested that his field observations conflicted with the "received understandings concerning its Indian origins".[[129]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Creese2016p305-131) In Bali, the Shudra (locally spelt _Soedra_) has typically been the temple priests, though depending on the demographics, a temple priest may also be from the other three castes.[[130]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-belo4-132) In most regions, it has been the Shudra who typically make offerings to the gods on behalf of the Hindu devotees, chant prayers, recite _meweda_ (Vedas), and set the course of Balinese temple festivals.[[130]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-belo4-132)
### Religion
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Religion")]
Main article: [Balinese Hinduism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_Hinduism "Balinese Hinduism")
Religion in Bali (2024)[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-auto-9)
  1. [Hinduism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") (86.4%)
  2. [Islam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islam "Islam") (10.3%)
  3. [Christianity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (2.62%)
  4. [Buddhism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") (0.68%)
  5. other (0.02%)


About 86.40% of Bali's population adheres to [Balinese Hinduism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_Hinduism "Balinese Hinduism"), formed as a combination of existing [local beliefs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_mythology "Balinese mythology") and [Hindu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") influences from mainland Southeast Asia and South Asia. Minority religions include [Islam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islam "Islam") (10.16%), [Christianity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (2.55%) (1.76%: [Protestantism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"); 0.86%: [Catholicism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism")), and [Buddhism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") (0.68%) as for 2022.[[131]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-AGAMA-133)[[132]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-RELIGION-134)
The [Balinese people](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_people "Balinese people") and [Bali Aga](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_Aga "Bali Aga") are generally [Hindu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") with some being [Christian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christian "Christian") and [Muslim](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Muslim "Muslim"). The [Javanese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Javanese_people "Javanese people"), [Sundanese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sundanese_people "Sundanese people"), [Sasak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sasak_people "Sasak people"), and [Malay](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malay_people "Malay people") people are generally [Muslim](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islam "Islam") and some are also Hindu or Christian.[[133]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-135) Meanwhile, people from [East Nusa Tenggara](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/East_Nusa_Tenggara "East Nusa Tenggara"), as well as people of the [Papua](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_New_Guinea "Western New Guinea"), [Batak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Batak_people "Batak people"), [Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_Indonesians "Chinese Indonesians") and some other Indonesian ethnic groups that aren't traditionally Muslim are generally Christian, with small minority are Hindu and Muslim. The village of Blimbing Sari in the Melaya district of [Jembrana Regency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jembrana_Regency "Jembrana Regency"), not far from [Gilimanuk Port](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Gilimanuk_Port&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gilimanuk Port \(page does not exist\)") [[id](https://id.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pelabuhan_Gilimanuk "id:Pelabuhan Gilimanuk")], is Christian, and the residents there are native Balinese, but the church is shaped like a Hindu temple.[[134]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-136)
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Besakih_Bali_Indonesia_Pura-Besakih-03.jpg/250px-Besakih_Bali_Indonesia_Pura-Besakih-03.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Besakih_Bali_Indonesia_Pura-Besakih-03.jpg)The [Mother Temple of Besakih](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Besakih_Temple "Besakih Temple"), one of Bali's most significant [Hindu temples](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindu_temple "Hindu temple").[[135]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-137)
The general beliefs and practices of _Agama Hindu Dharma_ mix ancient traditions and contemporary pressures placed by Indonesian laws that permit only monotheist belief under the national ideology of [_Pancasila_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pancasila_\(politics\) "Pancasila \(politics\)").[[136]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-junemcdaniel-138)[[137]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-syamashita-139) Traditionally, Hinduism in Indonesia had a pantheon of deities and that tradition of belief continues in practice; further, Hinduism in Indonesia granted freedom and flexibility to Hindus as to when, how and where to pray.[[137]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-syamashita-139) However, officially, the Indonesian government considers and advertises Indonesian Hinduism as a [monotheistic religion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monotheistic_religion "Monotheistic religion") with certain officially recognised beliefs that comply with its national ideology.[[136]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-junemcdaniel-138)[[137]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-syamashita-139)[[138]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-140) Indonesian school textbooks describe Hinduism as having one supreme being, Hindus offering three daily mandatory prayers, and Hinduism as having certain common beliefs that in part parallel those of Islam.[[137]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-syamashita-139)[[139]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-jmcdaniel2013-141) Some scholars[[137]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-syamashita-139)[[140]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-142)[[141]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-143)[_[who?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")_] contest whether these Indonesian government recognised and assigned beliefs to reflect the traditional beliefs and practices of Hindus in Indonesia before Indonesia gained independence from Dutch colonial rule. 
Balinese Hinduism has roots in Indian Hinduism and Buddhism, which arrived through [Java](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Java "Java").[[142]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-144) Hindu influences reached the [Indonesian Archipelago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Indonesia "List of islands of Indonesia") as early as the first century.[[143]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-jgonda-145) Historical evidence is unclear about the diffusion process of cultural and spiritual ideas from India. Java legends refer to Saka-era, traced to [78 AD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/78_AD "78 AD"). Stories from the [Mahabharata](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata") Epic have been traced in Indonesian islands to the 1st century; however, the versions mirror those found in the southeast Indian peninsular region (now [Tamil Nadu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu") and southern [Karnataka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka") and [Andhra Pradesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh")).[[143]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-jgonda-145)
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Pura_Bratan_Bali.jpg/250px-Pura_Bratan_Bali.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Pura_Bratan_Bali.jpg)[Pura Ulun Danu Bratan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pura_Ulun_Danu_Bratan "Pura Ulun Danu Bratan")
The Bali tradition adopted the pre-existing animistic traditions of the indigenous people. This influence strengthened the belief that the gods and goddesses are present in all things. Every element of nature, therefore, possesses its power, which reflects the power of the gods. A rock, tree, dagger, or woven cloth is a potential home for spirits whose energy can be directed for good or evil. Balinese Hinduism is deeply interwoven with art and ritual. Ritualising states of self-control are a notable feature of religious expression among the people, who for this reason have become famous for their graceful and decorous behavior.[[144]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-146)
Apart from the majority of Balinese Hindus, there also exist [Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_Chinese "Balinese Chinese") immigrants whose traditions have melded with that of the locals. As a result, these Sino-Balinese embrace their original religion, which is a mixture of Buddhism, Christianity, Taoism, and Confucianism, and find a way to harmonise it with the local traditions. Hence, it is not uncommon to find local Sino-Balinese during the local temple's _[odalan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Odalan "Odalan")_. Moreover, Balinese Hindu priests are invited to perform rites alongside a Chinese priest in the event of the death of a Sino-Balinese. Nevertheless, the Sino-Balinese claim to embrace Buddhism for administrative purposes, such as their Identity Cards.[[145]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-147) The [Roman Catholic community](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Indonesia "Catholic Church in Indonesia") has a [diocese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diocese "Diocese"), the [Diocese of Denpasar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Denpasar "Roman Catholic Diocese of Denpasar") that encompasses the province of Bali and [West Nusa Tenggara](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Nusa_Tenggara "West Nusa Tenggara") and has its [cathedral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral") located in [Denpasar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Denpasar "Denpasar"). 
  * [![Penataran Lempuyang Temple, Gunung Lempuyang, Bali](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Pura_Penataran_Lempuyang_Bali_492102459.jpg/120px-Pura_Penataran_Lempuyang_Bali_492102459.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Pura_Penataran_Lempuyang_Bali_492102459.jpg "Penataran Lempuyang Temple, Gunung Lempuyang, Bali")
_Penataran Lempuyang_ Temple, Gunung Lempuyang, Bali
  * [![Saint Joseph's Church, Denpasar](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/DenpasarSt.JosephChurch.JPG/120px-DenpasarSt.JosephChurch.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:DenpasarSt.JosephChurch.JPG "Saint Joseph's Church, Denpasar")
Saint Joseph's Church, Denpasar

### Languages
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Languages")]
Main article: [Balinese language](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_language "Balinese language")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Balinese_greetings_on_a_class_entrance.JPG/250px-Balinese_greetings_on_a_class_entrance.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Balinese_greetings_on_a_class_entrance.JPG)Balinese greetings "Om Swastiastu, Semoga Rahayu" in [Balinese script](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_script "Balinese script"), [Latin script](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") and [Devanagari](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") in one of the schools in Denpasar
[Balinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_language "Balinese language") and [Indonesian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") are the most widely spoken languages in Bali, and the vast majority of [Balinese people](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_people "Balinese people") are [bilingual](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bilingual "Bilingual") or [trilingual](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trilingual "Trilingual"). The most common spoken language around the tourist areas is Indonesian, as many people in the tourist sector are not solely Balinese, but migrants from [Java](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Java "Java"), [Lombok](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lombok "Lombok"), [Sumatra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra"), and other parts of Indonesia. The Balinese language is heavily [stratified](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Register_\(sociolinguistics\) "Register \(sociolinguistics\)") due to the [Balinese caste system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_caste_system "Balinese caste system").[[146]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-148) [Kawi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kawi_language "Kawi language") and [Sanskrit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") are also commonly used by some [Hindu priests](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindu_priest "Hindu priest") in Bali, as [Hindu literature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindu_literature "Hindu literature") was mostly written in Sanskrit.
[Balinese language](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_language "Balinese language")
[English](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/English_language "English language") and Chinese are the next most common languages (and the primary foreign languages) of many Balinese, owing to the requirements of the [tourism industry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tourism_industry "Tourism industry"), as well as the English-speaking community and huge Chinese-Indonesian population. Other foreign languages, such as [Japanese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"), [Korean](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"), [French](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/French_language "French language"), [Russian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") or [German](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/German_language "German language"), are often used in multilingual signs for foreign tourists. 
## Culture
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Culture")]
See also: [Balinese architecture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_architecture "Balinese architecture"), [Balinese art](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_art "Balinese art"), [Balinese cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_cuisine "Balinese cuisine"), [Balinese dance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_dance "Balinese dance"), and [Music of Bali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Music_of_Bali "Music of Bali")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Bali_cuisine.jpg/250px-Bali_cuisine.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Bali_cuisine.jpg)[Balinese cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_cuisine "Balinese cuisine")
Bali is renowned for its diverse and sophisticated art forms, such as painting, sculpture, woodcarving, handcrafts, and performing arts. [Balinese cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_cuisine "Balinese cuisine") is also distinctive, and unlike the rest of Indonesia, pork is commonly found in Balinese dishes such as [Babi Guling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pig_roast "Pig roast").[[147]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-149) Balinese percussion orchestra music, known as _[gamelan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gamelan "Gamelan")_ , is highly developed and varied. Balinese performing arts often portray stories from Hindu epics such as the [Ramayana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana") but with heavy Balinese influence. Famous [Balinese dances](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_dance "Balinese dance") include _[pendet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pendet "Pendet")_ , _[legong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legong "Legong")_ , _[baris](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baris_\(dance\) "Baris \(dance\)")_ , _[topeng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Topeng "Topeng")_ , _[barong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barong_\(mythology\) "Barong \(mythology\)")_ , _[gong kebyar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gamelan_gong_kebyar "Gamelan gong kebyar")_ , and _[kecak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kecak "Kecak")_ (the monkey dance). Bali boasts one of the most diverse and innovative performing arts cultures in the world, with paid performances at thousands of temple festivals, private ceremonies, and public shows.[[148]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-150)
In 2018 Governor [I Wayan Koster](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/I_Wayan_Koster "I Wayan Koster") issued Bali Governor's Regulation No. 79 of 2018 which mandated that city officials wear traditional Balinese dress, such as that made of _[songket](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Songket "Songket")_. This was followed by Circular No. 4 of 2021 which specified the use of [Endek fabrics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_textiles#Endek "Balinese textiles"), and was expanded to high-ranking individuals in the private sector and other institutions.[[149]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-151)
### Architecture
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Architecture")]
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Ulun_Temple_on_Lake_Bratan_%287609679414%29.jpg/250px-Ulun_Temple_on_Lake_Bratan_%287609679414%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Ulun_Temple_on_Lake_Bratan_\(7609679414\).jpg)The [pagoda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pagoda "Pagoda")-like [Pelinggih Meru](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meru_tower "Meru tower") shrine of [Pura Ulun Danu Bratan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pura_Ulun_Danu_Bratan "Pura Ulun Danu Bratan") is a distinctive feature of a Balinese temple.
_Kaja_ and _kelod_ are the Balinese equivalents of North and South, which refer to one's orientation between the island's largest mountain Gunung Agung (_kaja_), and the sea (_kelod_). In addition to spatial orientation, _kaja_ and _kelod_ have the connotation of good and evil; gods and ancestors are believed to live on the mountain whereas demons live in the sea. Buildings such as temples and residential homes are spatially oriented by having the most sacred spaces closest to the mountain and the unclean places nearest to the sea.[[150]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Herbst-152)[[151]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-153)
Most temples have an inner courtyard and an outer courtyard which are arranged with the inner courtyard furthest _kaja_. These spaces serve as performance venues since most Balinese rituals are accompanied by any combination of music, dance, and drama. The performances that take place in the inner courtyard are classified as _wali_ , the most sacred rituals which are offerings exclusively for the gods, while the outer courtyard is where _bebali_ ceremonies are held, which are intended for gods and people. Lastly, performances meant solely for the entertainment of humans take place outside the temple's walls and are called _bali-balihan_. This three-tiered system of classification was standardised in 1971 by a committee of Balinese officials and artists to better protect the sanctity of the oldest and most sacred Balinese rituals from being performed for a paying audience.[[152]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-154)
### Dances
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Dances")]
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Rejang_Adat.jpg/250px-Rejang_Adat.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Rejang_Adat.jpg)_[Rejang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rejang_dance "Rejang dance")_ , a sacred Balinese dance to greet the gods that come down to the earth on the ceremony day
Tourism, Bali's chief industry, has provided the island with a foreign audience that is eager to pay for entertainment, thus creating new performance opportunities and more demand for performers. The impact of [tourism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tourism_in_indonesia "Tourism in indonesia") is controversial since before it became integrated into the economy, the Balinese performing arts did not exist as a capitalist venture, and were not performed for entertainment outside of their respective ritual context. Since the 1930s sacred rituals such as the _[barong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barong_\(mythology\) "Barong \(mythology\)")_ [dance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_dance "Balinese dance") have been performed both in their original contexts, as well as exclusively for paying tourists. This has led to new versions of many of these performances that have developed according to the preferences of foreign audiences; some villages have a _barong_ mask specifically for non-ritual performances and an older mask that is only used for sacred performances.[[153]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Sanger-155)
### Festivals
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Festivals")]
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Lomba_Ogoh-ogoh_Kuta_2018.jpg/250px-Lomba_Ogoh-ogoh_Kuta_2018.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Lomba_Ogoh-ogoh_Kuta_2018.jpg)Ogoh-ogoh Festival in Kuta in 2018
Throughout the year, there are many festivals celebrated locally or island-wide according to the traditional calendars.[[154]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-156) The Hindu New Year, _[Nyepi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nyepi "Nyepi")_ , is celebrated in the spring by a day of silence. On this day everyone stays at home and tourists are encouraged (or required) to remain in their hotels. On the day before New Year, large and colourful sculptures of _[Ogoh-ogoh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ogoh-ogoh "Ogoh-ogoh")_ monsters are paraded and burned in the evening to drive away evil spirits. Other festivals throughout the year are specified by the Balinese _[pawukon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pawukon "Pawukon")_ [calendrical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calendar "Calendar") system. 
Celebrations are held for many occasions such as a tooth-filing (coming-of-age ritual), [cremation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ngaben "Ngaben") or _odalan_ (temple festival). One of the most important concepts that Balinese ceremonies have in common is that of _désa kala patra_ , which refers to how ritual performances must be appropriate in both the specific and general social context.[[150]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Herbst-152) Many ceremonial art forms such as _[wayang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wayang "Wayang") kulit_ and _[topeng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Topeng "Topeng")_ are highly improvisatory, providing flexibility for the performer to adapt the performance to the current situation.[[155]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-Foley-157) Many celebrations call for a loud, boisterous atmosphere with much activity, and the resulting aesthetic, _ramé_ , is distinctively Balinese. Often two or more _[gamelan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Music_of_Bali "Music of Bali")_ ensembles will be performing well within earshot, and sometimes compete with each other to be heard. Likewise, the audience members talk amongst themselves, get up and walk around, or even cheer on the performance, which adds to the many layers of activity and the liveliness typical of _ramé_.[[156]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-158)
### Tradition
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Tradition")]
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Ngaben_di_Nusa_Penida.jpg/250px-Ngaben_di_Nusa_Penida.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Ngaben_di_Nusa_Penida.jpg)[Cremation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ngaben "Ngaben") ceremony in [Nusa Penida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Penida "Nusa Penida")
Balinese society continues to revolve around each family's ancestral village, to which the cycle of life and religion is closely tied.[[157]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-NYT-159) Coercive aspects of traditional society, such as [customary law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Customary_law "Customary law") sanctions imposed by traditional authorities such as village councils (including "[kasepekang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kasepekang "Kasepekang")", or [shunning](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shunning "Shunning")) have risen in importance as a consequence of the democratisation and decentralisation of Indonesia since 1998.[[157]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-NYT-159)
Other than Balinese sacred rituals and festivals, the government presents [Bali Arts Festival](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_Arts_Festival "Bali Arts Festival") to showcase Bali's performing arts and various artworks produced by the local talents that they have. It is held once a year, from the second week of June until the end of July. Southeast Asia's biggest annual festival of words and ideas [Ubud Writers and Readers Festival](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ubud_Writers_and_Readers_Festival "Ubud Writers and Readers Festival") is held at [Ubud](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ubud "Ubud") in October, which is participated by the world's most celebrated writers, artists, thinkers, and performers.[[158]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-160)
One unusual tradition is the naming of children in Bali. In general, Balinese people [name their children](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balinese_name#Birth_order "Balinese name") depending on the order they are born, and the names are the same for both males and females. 
### Beauty pageant
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Beauty pageant")]
Bali was the host of [Miss World 2013](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miss_World_2013 "Miss World 2013") (63rd edition of the Miss World pageant). It was the first time Indonesia hosted an international beauty pageant. In 2022, Bali also co-hosted [Miss Grand International 2022](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miss_Grand_International_2022 "Miss Grand International 2022") along with Jakarta, West Java, and Banten. 
## International conferences and summits
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: International conferences and summits")]
[![icon](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/OOjs_UI_icon_robot.svg/40px-OOjs_UI_icon_robot.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:OOjs_UI_icon_robot.svg) | This section **may incorporate text from a[large language model](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:LLM "Wikipedia:LLM")**. Bali has hosted milestone regional and sectoral gatherings, including [ASEAN](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN")'s foundational summits and declarations, namely the 1976 Declaration of ASEAN Concord and Treaty of Amity and Cooperation in Southeast Asia, the Bali Concord II (2003), and the Bali Concord III (2011) alongside the East Asia Summit's "Bali Principles".[[159]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-161)[[160]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-162) It also hosted the 2007 [UNFCCC COP13](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2007_United_Nations_Climate_Change_Conference "2007 United Nations Climate Change Conference") that produced the Bali Road Map, as well as the [APEC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Economic_Cooperation "Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation") Leaders' Week and the [WTO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") Ninth Ministerial that adopted the Bali Package on trade facilitation.[[161]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-163)[[162]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-164)
Recurring platforms such as the Bali Process on people smuggling[[163]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-165) and the [Bali Democracy Forum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_Democracy_Forum "Bali Democracy Forum") have further anchored Bali's role in regional governance architectures.[[164]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-166) Sectoral leadership is also evident: the UN's Global Platform for Disaster Risk Reduction convened in Bali in 2022.[[165]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-167) Bali has also hosted the Multilateral Naval Exercise Komodo; according to [Reuters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reuters "Reuters"),[[166]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-168) the exercise has included participation by the [People's Liberation Army Navy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army_Navy "People's Liberation Army Navy") and taken place amid [South China Sea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea") tensions linked to overlapping claims, including China's "[nine-dash line](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nine-dash_line "Nine-dash line")", which an arbitral tribunal constituted under [UNCLOS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_Convention_on_the_Law_of_the_Sea "United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea") found had no legal basis in 2016, while official reports note that the 2025 iteration in Bali involved 38 countries and 19 foreign warships at [Tanjung Benoa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tanjung_Benoa "Tanjung Benoa").[[167]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-169)[[168]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-170)
## Sports
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Sports")]
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Stadion_Dipta.jpg/250px-Stadion_Dipta.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Stadion_Dipta.jpg)[Kapten I Wayan Dipta Stadium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kapten_I_Wayan_Dipta_Stadium "Kapten I Wayan Dipta Stadium"), the home of [Bali United F.C.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_United_F.C. "Bali United F.C.")
Bali is a major world [surfing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surfing "Surfing") destination with popular breaks dotted across the southern coastline and around the offshore island of [Nusa Lembongan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Lembongan "Nusa Lembongan").[[169]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-171)
As part of the [Coral Triangle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coral_Triangle "Coral Triangle"), Bali, including [Nusa Penida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nusa_Penida "Nusa Penida"), offers a wide range of dive sites with varying types of reefs, and tropical aquatic life. 
Bali was the host of [2008 Asian Beach Games](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008_Asian_Beach_Games "2008 Asian Beach Games").[[170]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-172) It was the second time Indonesia hosted an Asia-level [multi-sport event](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event"), after [Jakarta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") held the [1962 Asian Games](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1962_Asian_Games "1962 Asian Games"). 
In 2023, Bali was the location for a major [eSports](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Esports "Esports") event, the [Dota 2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dota_2 "Dota 2") Bali Major, the third and final Major of the [Dota Pro Circuit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dota_Pro_Circuit "Dota Pro Circuit") season. The event was held at the Ayana Estate and the Champa Garden, and it was the first time that a Dota Pro Circuit Major was held in Indonesia.[[171]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-173)
In [football](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), Bali is home to [Bali United](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali_United "Bali United") football club, which plays in [Liga 1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liga_1_\(Indonesia\) "Liga 1 \(Indonesia\)"). The team was relocated from [Samarinda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samarinda "Samarinda"), [East Kalimantan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/East_Kalimantan "East Kalimantan") to [Gianyar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gianyar "Gianyar"), Bali. Harbiansyah Hanafiah, the main commissioner of Bali United explained that he changed the name and moved the home base because there was no representative from Bali in the highest football tier in Indonesia.[[172]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-174) Another reason was due to local fans in Samarinda preferring to support [Pusamania Borneo F.C.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pusamania_Borneo_F.C. "Pusamania Borneo F.C.") rather than Persisam.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]
## Heritage sites
[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bali&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Heritage sites")]
In June 2012, [Subak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subak_\(irrigation\) "Subak \(irrigation\)"), the irrigation system for [paddy fields](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paddy_field "Paddy field") in [Jatiluwih](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jatiluwih "Jatiluwih"), central Bali was listed as a Natural [UNESCO World Heritage Site](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site").[[173]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bali#cite_note-175)

**Indian cuisine** consists of a variety of regional and traditional cuisines native to the [Indian subcontinent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"). Given the diversity in soil, climate, culture, ethnic groups, and occupations, these cuisines vary substantially and use locally available ingredients. 
Indian food is also heavily influenced by religion, cultural choices and traditions.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Dias1996-1)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-GestelandGesteland2010-2) Historical events such as invasions, trade relations, and [colonialism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism") have played a role in introducing certain foods to India. The [Columbian discovery](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Columbian_exchange "Columbian exchange") of the [New World](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_World "New World") brought a number of new vegetables and fruits. A number of these such as [potatoes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Potato "Potato"), [tomatoes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tomato "Tomato"), [chillies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chili_pepper "Chili pepper"), [peanuts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peanut "Peanut"), and [guava](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guava "Guava") have become staples in many regions of India.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-3)
Indian cuisine has shaped the history of [international relations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_relations "International relations"); the [spice trade](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spice_trade "Spice trade") between India and [Europe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Europe "Europe") was the primary catalyst for Europe's [Age of Discovery](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery").[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-cornillez1999-4) Spices were bought from India and traded around Europe and Asia. Indian cuisine has influenced other cuisines across the world, especially those from [Europe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_cuisine "European cuisine") ([Britain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_cuisine "British cuisine") in particular), the [Middle East](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Middle_Eastern_cuisine "Middle Eastern cuisine"), [Southern Africa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/African_cuisine#Southern_African "African cuisine"), [East Africa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/East_African_cuisine "East African cuisine"), [Southeast Asia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Southeast_Asian_cuisine "Southeast Asian cuisine"), [North America](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_American_cuisine "North American cuisine"), [Mauritius](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mauritian_cuisine "Mauritian cuisine"), [Fiji](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fijian_cuisine "Fijian cuisine"), [Oceania](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oceanic_cuisine "Oceanic cuisine"), and the [Caribbean](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Caribbean_cuisine "Caribbean cuisine").[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-vegvoyages.com-5)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-6)
[World Wildlife Fund](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Wide_Fund_for_Nature "World Wide Fund for Nature") (WWF)’s [Living Planet Report](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Living_Planet_Report "Living Planet Report") released on 10 October 2024 emphasised India’s food consumption pattern as the most sustainable among the big economies ([G20 countries](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/G20 "G20")).[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-7)
## History
Main article: [History of Indian cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_Indian_cuisine "History of Indian cuisine")
Indian cuisine reflects an [8,000-year history](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_India "History of India") of various groups and cultures interacting with the [Indian subcontinent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), leading to diversity of flavours and regional cuisines found in modern-day India. Later, trade with [British](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_people "British people") and [Portuguese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portuguese_people "Portuguese people") influence added to the already diverse Indian cuisine.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Dubey2011-8)[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Achaya-9)
### Prehistory and Indus Valley Civilisation
[![icon](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/60px-Question_book-new.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not[cite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. See also: [Meluhha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meluhha "Meluhha"), [Indus–Mesopotamia relations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indus%E2%80%93Mesopotamia_relations "Indus–Mesopotamia relations"), and [Indian maritime history](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_maritime_history "Indian maritime history")
After 9000 BCE, a first period of indirect contacts between [Fertile Crescent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fertile_Crescent "Fertile Crescent") and [Indus Valley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") civilisations seems to have occurred as a consequence of the [Neolithic Revolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution") and the diffusion of agriculture. Wheat and barley were first grown around 7000 BCE, when agriculture spread from the Fertile Crescent to the Indus Valley. [Sesame](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sesame "Sesame") and [humped cattle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zebu "Zebu") were domesticated in the local farming communities. Mehrgarh is one of the earliest sites with evidence of farming and herding in [South Asia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"). By 3000 BCE, [turmeric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turmeric "Turmeric"), [cardamom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cardamom "Cardamom"), [black pepper](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_pepper "Black pepper") and [mustard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mustard_plant "Mustard plant") were harvested in India. 
From around 2350 BCE evidence for imports from the Indus to [Ur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ur "Ur") in [Mesopotamia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") has been found, as well as [Clove](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clove "Clove") heads thought to originate from the [Moluccas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moluccas "Moluccas") in [Maritime Southeast Asia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maritime_Southeast_Asia "Maritime Southeast Asia"), which were found in a 2nd millennium BC site in [Terqa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Terqa "Terqa"). [Akkadian Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Akkadian_Empire "Akkadian Empire") records mention timber, carnelian and ivory as being imported from [Meluhha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meluhha "Meluhha") by Meluhhan ships, Meluhha being generally considered as the Mesopotamian name for the Indus Valley Civilisation.
