The ancient [Hindu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") text _[Mahabharata](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata")_ mentions rice and vegetable cooked together, and the word "pulao" or "pallao" is used to refer to the dish in ancient [Sanskrit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") works, such as [Yājñavalkya Smṛti](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Y%C4%81j%C3%B1avalkya_Sm%E1%B9%9Bti "Yājñavalkya Smṛti"). [Ayurveda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ayurveda "Ayurveda"), ancient Indian system of wellness, deals with holistic approach to the wellness, and it includes food, [dhyana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dhyana_in_Hinduism "Dhyana in Hinduism") (meditation) and [yoga](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yoga "Yoga"). 
### Antiquity
Early diet in India mainly consisted of [legumes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legume "Legume"), [vegetables](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vegetable "Vegetable"), [fruits](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fruit "Fruit"), [grains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grain "Grain"), [dairy products](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dairy_product "Dairy product"), and [honey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honey "Honey").[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-10) Staple foods eaten today include a variety of lentils (_[dal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dal "Dal")_), [whole-wheat flour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whole-wheat_flour "Whole-wheat flour") (_aṭṭa_), rice, and [pearl millet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pearl_millet "Pearl millet") (_bājra_), which has been cultivated in the Indian subcontinent since 6200 BCE.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Achaya-9) The Sangam literature, which is specific to South India, mentions that fish, crab, forest cattle, pork, monitor lizard, and poultry were consumed in the region together with a variety of millets, sago, sugarcane, dairy products, honey, and rice.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-11)
Over time, segments of the population embraced [vegetarianism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vegetarianism "Vegetarianism") during the [Śramaṇa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C5%9Arama%E1%B9%87a "Śramaṇa") movement[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-12)[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-13) while an equitable climate permitted a variety of fruits, vegetables, and grains to be grown throughout the year. 
A food classification system that categorised any item as _[saatvic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Satvic "Satvic")_ , _[raajsic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajasic "Rajasic")_ , or _[taamsic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tamasic "Tamasic")_ developed in [Yoga](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yoga "Yoga") tradition.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-14)[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-15) The _[Bhagavad Gita](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bhagavad_Gita "Bhagavad Gita")_ proscribes certain dietary practices (chapter 17, verses 8–10).[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-16)
Consumption of [beef](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beef "Beef") is [taboo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taboo_food_and_drink#Cattle "Taboo food and drink"), due to cows being considered sacred in Hinduism.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Sharpes2006-17) Beef is generally not eaten by Hindus in India except for [Kerala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"), parts of southern [Tamil Nadu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu") and the north-east.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-18)
### Ingredients mentioned in ancient Indian scripture
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Pomegranate_fruit_-_whole_and_piece_with_arils.jpg/330px-Pomegranate_fruit_-_whole_and_piece_with_arils.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Pomegranate_fruit_-_whole_and_piece_with_arils.jpg)Pomegranate
While many ancient Indian recipes have been lost in history, one can look at ancient texts to see what was eaten in ancient and pre-historic India. 
  * [Barley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barley#Dispersal "Barley")[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-sacred-texts1-19)—(known as _Yava_ in both [Vedic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit "Vedic Sanskrit") and [Classical Sanskrit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classical_Sanskrit "Classical Sanskrit")) is mentioned many times in [Rigveda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rigveda "Rigveda") and other Indian scriptures as one of the principal grains in ancient India
  * [Betel leaf](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Betel#Usage_and_cultural_significance "Betel")[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-20)—primary use is as a wrapper for the chewing of [areca nut](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Areca_nut "Areca nut") or [tobacco](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco"), where it is mainly used to add flavour; may also be used in cooking, usually raw, for its peppery taste
  * [Breadfruit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Breadfruit#South_Asia "Breadfruit")—[fritters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fritters "Fritters") called _jeev kadge phodi_ in [Konkani](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Konkani_language "Konkani language")[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-21) or _kadachakka varuthath_[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-22) in [Malayalam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") are a local delicacy in coastal [Karnataka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka") and [Kerala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kerala "Kerala")
  * [Chickpeas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chickpea#Culinary "Chickpea")[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Carr-23)—popular dishes are made with chickpea flour, such as _[mirchi bajji](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mirchi_bajji "Mirchi bajji")_ and _mirapakaya bajji_
  * [Curd](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curd_\(India\) "Curd \(India\)")—a traditional yogurt or fermented milk product, originating from the Indian subcontinent, usually prepared from cow's milk, and sometimes buffalo milk, or goat milk[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]*[Figs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Common_fig#From_ancient_times "Common fig")[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-sacred-texts1-19)—cultivated from [Afghanistan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") to [Portugal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), also grown in [Pithoragarh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pithoragarh "Pithoragarh") in the [Kumaon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kumaon_Mastiff "Kumaon Mastiff") hills of [India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/India "India"); from the 15th century onwards, also grown in areas including Northern [Europe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Europe "Europe") and the [New World](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_World "New World")
  * [Ghee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ghee "Ghee")—a class of clarified butter that originated in ancient India, commonly used in the Indian subcontinent, Middle-Eastern cuisine, traditional medicine, and religious rituals[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]*[Grape wine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wine "Wine")[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-archive1-24)—first-known mention of [grape](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grape "Grape")-based wines in India is from the late 4th-century BC writings of [Chanakya](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chanakya "Chanakya")
  * [Honey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honey#History "Honey")[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-25)—the spiritual and supposed therapeutic use of honey in [ancient India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_India#Bronze_Age_\(c._3300_%E2%80%93_c._1800_BCE\) "History of India") was documented in both the [Vedas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vedas "Vedas") and the [Ayurveda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ayurveda "Ayurveda") texts[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]*[Mango](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mango#Cultural_significance "Mango")—the [Jain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jain "Jain") goddess [Ambika](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ambika_\(Jainism\) "Ambika \(Jainism\)") is traditionally represented as sitting under a mango tree[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]
  * [Mustard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mustard_\(condiment\)#Indian_subcontinent "Mustard \(condiment\)")[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-sacred-texts1-19)—[brown mustard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brassica_juncea "Brassica juncea") is a spice that was cultivated in the [Indus Valley civilisation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indus_Valley_civilization "Indus Valley civilization") and is one of the important spices used in the Indian subcontinent today
  * [Pomegranate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pomegranate#Culinary_use "Pomegranate")—in some Hindu traditions, the pomegranate ([Hindi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"): _anār_) symbolises prosperity and fertility, and is associated with both [Bhoomidevi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bhumidevi "Bhumidevi") (the earth goddess) and Lord [Ganesha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganesha "Ganesha") (the one fond of the many-seeded fruit)
  * [Rice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_rice_cultivation#Indian_subcontinent "History of rice cultivation")—cultivated in the Indian subcontinent from as early as 5,000 BC
  * [Rice cake](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rice_cake#Indian "Rice cake")—quite a variety are available[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-26)[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]*[Rose apple](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syzygium_samarangense "Syzygium samarangense")—mainly eaten as a fruit and also used to make pickles (_chambakka achar_)
  * [Saffron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saffron#Consumption "Saffron")[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-sacred-texts2-27)—almost all saffron grows in a belt from Spain in the west to [Kashmir](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir") in the east
  * [Salt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salt#In_religion "Salt")[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-sacred-texts2-27)—considered to be a very auspicious substance in [Hinduism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") and is used in particular religious ceremonies like house-warmings and weddings; in [Jainism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"), devotees lay an offering of raw rice with a pinch of salt before a deity to signify their devotion, and salt is sprinkled on a person's cremated remains before the ashes are buried
  * [Sesame oil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sesame_oil "Sesame oil")[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-sacred-texts2-27)—popular in Asia, especially in [Korea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Korea "Korea"), [China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/China "China"), and the South Indian states of [Karnataka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka"), [Andhra Pradesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh"), and [Tamil Nadu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu"), where its widespread use is similar to that of [olive oil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil") in the Mediterranean[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]*[Sorghum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sorghum_bicolor#Culinary_use "Sorghum bicolor")[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Carr-23)—commonly called _jwaarie_ , _jowar_ , _jola_ , or _jondhalaa_ , sorghum is one of the staple sources of nutrition
  * [Sugar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sugar#Asia "Sugar")—produced in the Indian subcontinent since ancient times, its cultivation spread from there into modern-day Afghanistan through the Khyber Pass
  * [Sugarcane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sugarcane#History "Sugarcane")[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Carr-23)—the earliest known production of crystalline sugar began in northern India; the earliest evidence of sugar production comes from ancient Sanskrit and Pali texts
  * [Turmeric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Golden_milk "Golden milk")[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-archive1-24)—used widely as a spice in South Asian and Middle Eastern cooking


### Middle Ages to the 16th century
During the [Middle Ages](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medieval_India "Medieval India"), several Indian dynasties were predominant, including the [Gupta dynasty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gupta_dynasty "Gupta dynasty"). Travel to India during this time introduced new cooking methods and products to the region, including [tea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tea "Tea").[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]
India was later invaded by tribes from [Central Asian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") cultures, which led to the emergence of [Mughlai cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mughlai_cuisine "Mughlai cuisine"), a mix of Indian and [Central Asian cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Asian_cuisine "Central Asian cuisine"). Hallmarks include seasonings such as [saffron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saffron "Saffron").[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-LocricchioMcConnell2004-28)
### Colonial Period
The [Portuguese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India") and [British](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_India "British India") during their rule introduced cooking techniques such as baking, and foods from the [New World](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_World "New World") and Europe. The [new-world vegetables](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_World_crops "New World crops") popular in cuisine from the Indian subcontinent include [maize](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maize "Maize"), [tomato](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tomato "Tomato"), [potato](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Potato "Potato"), [sweet potatoes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sweet_potatoes "Sweet potatoes"), [peanuts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peanut "Peanut"), [squash](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Squash_\(plant\) "Squash \(plant\)"), and [chilli](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Capsicum "Capsicum"). Most New World vegetables such as sweet potatoes, potatoes, [Amaranth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amaranth "Amaranth"), peanuts and [cassava](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cassava "Cassava") based [Sago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sago "Sago") are allowed on Hindu fasting days. [Cauliflower](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cauliflower "Cauliflower") was introduced by the British in 1822.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-29) In the late 18th/early 19th century, an autobiography of a Scottish [Robert Lindsay](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Lindsay_\(Sylhet\) "Robert Lindsay \(Sylhet\)") mentions a [Sylheti](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sylhetis "Sylhetis") man called Saeed Ullah cooking a curry for Lindsay's family. This is possibly the oldest record of Indian cuisine in the [United Kingdom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-star-30)[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-autobio-31)

## Ingredients
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Spices_in_an_Indian_market.jpg/250px-Spices_in_an_Indian_market.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Spices_in_an_Indian_market.jpg)[Spices](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spice "Spice") at a grocery store in India
[Staple foods](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Staple_food "Staple food") of Indian cuisine include [pearl millet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pearl_millet "Pearl millet") (_bājra_), [rice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rice "Rice"), [whole-wheat flour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whole-wheat_flour "Whole-wheat flour") (_aṭṭa_), and a variety of [lentils](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lentil "Lentil"), such as _masoor_ (most often red [lentils](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lentil "Lentil")), _tuer_ ([pigeon peas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pigeon_pea "Pigeon pea")), _[urad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urad_\(bean\) "Urad \(bean\)")_ (black gram), and _moong_ ([mung beans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mung_bean "Mung bean")). Lentils may be used whole, dehusked—for example, _dhuli moong_ or _dhuli urad_ —or split. Split lentils, or _dal_ , are used extensively.[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Johnston1958-33) Some [pulses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pulse_\(legume\) "Pulse \(legume\)"), such as _channa_ or _cholae_ ([chickpeas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chickpea "Chickpea")), _[rajma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajma "Rajma")_ ([kidney beans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kidney_bean "Kidney bean")), and _lobiya_ ([black-eyed peas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black-eyed_pea "Black-eyed pea")) are very common, especially in the northern regions. _Channa_ and _moong_ are also processed into flour (_[besan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gram_flour "Gram flour")_). 
Many Indian dishes are cooked in [vegetable oil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vegetable_oil "Vegetable oil"), but [peanut oil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peanut_oil "Peanut oil") is popular in northern and western India, [mustard oil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mustard_oil "Mustard oil") in eastern India,[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-LocricchioMcConnell2004-28) and [coconut oil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coconut_oil "Coconut oil") along the western coast, especially in Kerala and parts of southern Tamil Nadu.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Seshadri2007-34)[_[self-published source?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")_] [_Gingelly_ (sesame) oil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sesame_oil "Sesame oil") is common in the south since it imparts a fragrant, nutty aroma.[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-ShiHo2010-35)
In recent decades, [sunflower](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunflower_oil "Sunflower oil"), [safflower](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Safflower "Safflower"), [cottonseed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cottonseed_oil "Cottonseed oil"), and [soybean](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soybean_oil "Soybean oil") oils have become popular across India.[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-KumarDubey2006-36) [Hydrogenated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogenation "Hydrogenation") vegetable oil, known as _[Vanaspati](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vanaspati "Vanaspati") ghee_, is another popular cooking medium.[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Sharma-37) Butter-based [ghee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ghee "Ghee"), or _deshi ghee_ , is used commonly. 
Many types of meat are used for Indian cooking, but chicken and [mutton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lamb_and_mutton "Lamb and mutton") tend to be the most commonly consumed meats. Fish and beef consumption are prevalent in some parts of India, but they are not widely consumed except for coastal areas, as well as the north east.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/3_types_of_lentil.jpg/250px-3_types_of_lentil.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:3_types_of_lentil.jpg)[Lentils](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lentil "Lentil") are a staple ingredient in Indian cuisine.
The most important and frequently used spices and flavourings in Indian cuisine are whole or powdered [chilli pepper](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chili_pepper "Chili pepper") (_mirch_ , [introduced by the Portuguese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Columbian_Exchange "Columbian Exchange") from [Mexico](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") in the 16th century), [black mustard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brassica_nigra "Brassica nigra") seed (_sarso_), [cardamom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cardamom "Cardamom") (_elaichi_), [cumin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cumin "Cumin") (_jeera_), [turmeric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turmeric "Turmeric") (_haldi_), [asafoetida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asafoetida "Asafoetida") (_hing_), [ginger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ginger "Ginger") (_adrak_), [coriander](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coriander "Coriander") (_dhania_), and [garlic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Garlic "Garlic") (_lasoon_).[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Kapoor2002-38)
One popular [spice mix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spice_mix "Spice mix") is _[garam masala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Garam_masala "Garam masala")_ , a powder that typically includes seven dried spices in a particular ratio, including [black cardamom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cardamom "Cardamom"), [cinnamon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cinnamon "Cinnamon") (_dalchini_), [clove](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clove "Clove") (_laung),_ cumin (jeera), black peppercorns, coriander seeds and [anise star](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Illicium_verum "Illicium verum").[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Kelley2009-39)[_[self-published source?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")_]. Each culinary region has a distinctive _garam masala_ blend—individual [chefs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chef "Chef") may also have their own. 
The spices chosen for a dish are freshly ground and then fried in hot oil or ghee to create a paste. The process is called _bhuna_ , the name also being used for a type of [curry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curry "Curry").[[40]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-40)
There are other spice blends which are popular in various regions. _[Panch phoron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Panch_phoron "Panch phoron")_ is a spice blend which is popular in eastern India. _Goda masala_ is a sweet spice mix which is popular in [Maharashtra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"). Some leaves commonly used for flavouring include [bay leaves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bay_leaf "Bay leaf") (_[tejpat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cinnamomum_tamala "Cinnamomum tamala")_), [coriander](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coriander "Coriander") leaves, [fenugreek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fenugreek "Fenugreek") (_[methi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Methi "Methi")_) leaves, and [mint](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mentha "Mentha") leaves. The use of [curry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curry_tree "Curry tree") leaves and roots for flavouring is typical of [Gujarati](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gujarati_cuisine "Gujarati cuisine")[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Gress2008-41) and [South Indian cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Indian_cuisine "South Indian cuisine").[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Sanmugam2007-42) Sweet dishes are often seasoned with cardamom, [saffron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saffron "Saffron"), [nutmeg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nutmeg "Nutmeg"), and [rose](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rose "Rose") petal essences. 
## Regional cuisines
See also: [List of Indian dishes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Indian_dishes "List of Indian dishes")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Indian_food_at_restaurant_in_Paris_-_2020-08-20.jpg/250px-Indian_food_at_restaurant_in_Paris_-_2020-08-20.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Indian_food_at_restaurant_in_Paris_-_2020-08-20.jpg)Indian food at restaurant in Paris.
Cuisine differs across [India's diverse regions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_India "Administrative divisions of India") as a result of variation in local culture, geographical location (proximity to sea, desert, or mountains), and economics. It also varies seasonally, depending on which fruits and vegetables are ripe. 
### Andaman and Nicobar Islands
[Seafood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seafood "Seafood") plays a major role in the cuisine of the [Andaman and Nicobar Islands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andaman_and_Nicobar_Islands "Andaman and Nicobar Islands").[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Andaman_and_Nicobar_Isles_cuisine-43) Staples of the diet of the Indigenous [Andamanese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andamanese_peoples "Andamanese peoples") traditionally include roots, honey, fruits, meat, and fish, obtained by hunting and gathering. Some insects were also eaten as delicacies.[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-44) Immigration from mainland of India, however, has resulted in variations in the cuisine. 
### Andhra Pradesh
Main article: [Andhra cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andhra_cuisine "Andhra cuisine")
[![icon](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/60px-Question_book-new.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for[verification](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Vegetarian_Andhra_Meal.jpg/250px-Vegetarian_Andhra_Meal.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Vegetarian_Andhra_Meal.jpg)A vegetarian [Andhra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh") meal served on important occasions
The cuisine of [Andhra Pradesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh") belongs to the two Telugu-speaking regions of [Rayalaseema](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rayalaseema "Rayalaseema") and Coastal Andhra and is part of [Telugu cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Telugu_cuisine "Telugu cuisine"). The food of Andhra Pradesh is known for its heavy use of spices, and the use of [tamarind](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tamarind "Tamarind"). 
Seafood is common in the coastal region of the state. Rice is the staple food (as is with all South Indian states) eaten with lentil preparations such as _pappu_ (lentils) and _pulusu_(stew) and spicy vegetables or curries. 
In Andhra, leafy greens or vegetables such as bottle-gourd and eggplant are usually added to _dal_. Pickles are an essential part of the local cuisine; popular among those are mango-based pickles such as _[avakaya](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Avakaya "Avakaya")_ and _maagaya_ , _[gongura](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gongura "Gongura")_ (a pickle made from [sorrel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sorrel "Sorrel") leaves),[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Andhra_food_delicacies-45) _usirikaya_ (gooseberry or _amla_),_nimmakaya_(lime), and tomato pickle. 
[_Perugu_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dahi_\(curd\) "Dahi \(curd\)") (yogurt) is a common addition to meals, as a way of tempering spiciness. Breakfast items include _[dosa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dosa_\(food\) "Dosa \(food\)"), pesarattu_ (mung bean _dosa_), [_vada_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vada_\(food\) "Vada \(food\)"), and _[idli](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Idli "Idli")_. 
### Arunachal Pradesh
Main article: [Cuisine of Arunachal Pradesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cuisine_of_Arunachal_Pradesh "Cuisine of Arunachal Pradesh")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Pitang_Oying_-_Mising_Kitchen_Resturant%2C_Guwahati%2C_Assam%2C_1618420295814._jpg.jpg/250px-Pitang_Oying_-_Mising_Kitchen_Resturant%2C_Guwahati%2C_Assam%2C_1618420295814._jpg.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Pitang_Oying_-_Mising_Kitchen_Resturant,_Guwahati,_Assam,_1618420295814._jpg.jpg)Pitang Oying
[Arunachal Pradesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arunachal_Pradesh "Arunachal Pradesh") is located in the northeast of India, bordered by Bhutan, Myanmar, and a disputed border with [Tibet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tibet_Autonomous_Region "Tibet Autonomous Region"). 
The staple food is rice, along with fish, meat, and [leaf vegetables](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leaf_vegetable "Leaf vegetable").[[46]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Arunachal_Pradesh_Food-46) Native tribes of Arunachal are meat eaters and use fish, eggs, beef, chicken, pork, and mutton to make their dishes. 
Many varieties of rice are used. Boiled rice cakes wrapped in leaves are a popular snack. _[Thukpa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thukpa "Thukpa")_ is a kind of noodle soup common among the [Monpa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monpa_people "Monpa people") tribe of the region.[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-North_east_cuisine-47)
[Lettuce](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lettuce "Lettuce") is the most common vegetable, usually prepared by [boiling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boiling "Boiling") with ginger, coriander, and green chillies.[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Arunachal_Pradesh_food-48)
_Apong_ or [rice beer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rice_beer "Rice beer") made from fermented rice or millet is a popular beverage in Arunachal Pradesh and is consumed as a refreshing drink.[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Thorpe-49)
### Assam
Main article: [Assamese cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assamese_cuisine "Assamese cuisine")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/A_lunch_platter_of_Assamese_cuisine.jpg/250px-A_lunch_platter_of_Assamese_cuisine.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:A_lunch_platter_of_Assamese_cuisine.jpg)A lunch platter of Assamese cuisine
[Assam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assam "Assam") is located in the northeast of India, bordered by Bhutan. Its cuisine is a mixture of different indigenous styles, with considerable regional variation and some external influences. Although it is known for its limited use of spices,[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Assam_General_Knowledge-50) Assamese cuisine has strong flavours from its use of endemic herbs, fruits, and vegetables served fresh, dried, or [fermented](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermentation_\(food\) "Fermentation \(food\)"). 
Rice is the staple food item and a huge variety of endemic rice varieties, including several varieties of sticky rice are a part of the cuisine in Assam. Fish, generally freshwater varieties, are widely eaten. Other non-vegetarian items include chicken, duck, squab, snails, silkworms, insects, goat, pork, venison, turtle, monitor lizard, etc. 
The region's cuisine involves simple cooking processes, mostly barbecuing, steaming, or boiling. Spices are not fried before use in the cuisine of Assam. 
A traditional meal in Assam begins with a _khar_ , a class of dishes named after the main ingredient and ends with a _tenga_ , a sour dish. Homebrewed rice beer or [rice wine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rice_wine "Rice wine") is served before a meal. The food is usually served in [bell metal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bell_metal "Bell metal") utensils.[[51]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Gupta1982-51) _[Paan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paan "Paan")_ , the practice of chewing [betel nut](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Betel_nut "Betel nut"), generally concludes a meal.[[52]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Penzer1980-52)
### West Bengal
Main article: [Bengali cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bengali_cuisine "Bengali cuisine")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Pithe_Puli_-_Home_-_Kolkata_-_West_Bengal.jpg/250px-Pithe_Puli_-_Home_-_Kolkata_-_West_Bengal.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Pithe_Puli_-_Home_-_Kolkata_-_West_Bengal.jpg)[Pithe Puli](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pitha "Pitha")
[West Bengal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal") is located in the eastern portion of India, along the [Bay of Bengal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal"). 
Mughal cuisine is a universal influencer in the Bengali palate, and has introduced Persian and Islamic foods to the region, as well as a number of more elaborate methods of preparing food, like marination using ghee. Fish, meat (chicken, goat meat), egg, rice, milk, and sugar all play crucial parts in Bengali cuisine.[[53]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-53)
Bengali cuisine can be subdivided into four different types of dishes, _charbya_ (চারব্য), or food that is chewed, such as rice or fish; _choṣya_ , or food that is sucked, such as _ambal_ and _tak_ ; _lehya_ (লেহ্য), or foods that are meant to be licked, like _chuttney_ ; and _peya_ (পেয়ে), which includes drinks, mainly milk.[[54]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-54)
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Shorshe_Pabda_%28Pabo_catfish_in_Mustard_paste%29-Home%2Ckolkata-West_Bengal-IMG_0001.jpg/250px-Shorshe_Pabda_%28Pabo_catfish_in_Mustard_paste%29-Home%2Ckolkata-West_Bengal-IMG_0001.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Shorshe_Pabda_\(Pabo_catfish_in_Mustard_paste\)-Home,kolkata-West_Bengal-IMG_0001.jpg)Shorshe Pabda ([Pabo catfish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ompok "Ompok") in mustard paste)
During the 19th century, many [Odia-speaking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Odia_people "Odia people") cooks were employed in [Bengal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"),[[55]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-55) which led to the transfer of several food items between the two regions. Bengali cuisine is the only traditionally developed multi-course tradition from the Indian subcontinent that is analogous in structure to the modern [service à la russe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Service_%C3%A0_la_russe "Service à la russe") style of French cuisine, with food served course-wise rather than all at once.[[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-56)
Bengali cuisine differs according to regional tastes, such as the emphasis on the use of [chilli pepper](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chili_pepper "Chili pepper") in the [Chittagong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chittagong "Chittagong") district of Bangladesh[[57]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-57) However, across all its varieties, there is predominant use of mustard oil along with large amounts of spices. 
The cuisine is known for subtle flavours with an emphasis on [fish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fish_as_food "Fish as food"), meat, vegetables, lentils, and rice.[[58]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Laveesh2009\(5\)-58) Bread is also a common dish in Bengali cuisine, particularly a deep-fried version called _[luchi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Luchi "Luchi")_ is popular. Fresh aquatic fish is one of its most distinctive features; Bengalis prepare fish in many ways, such as [steaming](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steaming "Steaming"), [braising](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Braising "Braising"), or [stewing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stew "Stew") in vegetables and sauces based on [coconut milk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coconut_milk "Coconut milk") or [mustard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mustard_\(condiment\) "Mustard \(condiment\)"). 
East Bengali food, which has a high presence in [West Bengal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal") and Bangladesh, is much spicier than the West Bengali cuisine, and tends to use high amounts of chilli, and is one of the spiciest cuisines in India and the World. 
[_Shondesh_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sandesh_\(confectionery\) "Sandesh \(confectionery\)") and _[Rashogolla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rasgulla "Rasgulla")_ are popular dishes made of sweetened, finely ground fresh cheese. For the latter, West Bengal and neighbouring [Odisha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Odisha "Odisha") both claim to be the origin of dessert. Each state also has a [geographical indication](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geographical_indication "Geographical indication") for their regional variety of _rasgulla_.[[59]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-rasgulla_1-59)[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-rasgulla_2-60)
The cuisine is also found in the state of [Tripura](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tripura "Tripura") and the [Barak Valley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barak_Valley "Barak Valley") of Assam. 
### Bihar
Main article: [Bihari cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bihari_cuisine "Bihari cuisine")
See also: [Angika cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angika_cuisine "Angika cuisine"), [Bhojpuri cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bhojpuri_cuisine "Bhojpuri cuisine"), [Maithil cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maithil_cuisine "Maithil cuisine"), and [Magahi cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magahi_cuisine "Magahi cuisine")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Litti_Chokha.jpg/250px-Litti_Chokha.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Litti_Chokha.jpg)Litti Chokha
Bihari cuisine may include _litti chokha_ ,[[61]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-61) a baked salted wheat-flour cake filled with _[sattu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sattu "Sattu")_ (baked chickpea flour) and some special spices, which is served with _baigan ke chokha_ ,[[62]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-62) made of roasted eggplant (_brinjal_) and tomatoes.[[63]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-HughesMookherjee2001-63)[[64]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Laveesh2009-64)
Among meat dishes, meat _saalan_[[65]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-65) is a popular dish made of mutton or goat curry with cubed potatoes in _[garam masala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Garam_masala "Garam masala")_. 
_Dalpuri_ is another popular dish in Bihar. It is salted wheat-flour bread, filled with boiled, crushed, and fried gram pulses.[[66]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-66)
_[Malpua](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malpua "Malpua")_ is a popular sweet dish of Bihar, prepared by a mixture of _[maida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maida_\(flour\) "Maida \(flour\)")_ , milk, bananas, cashew nuts, peanuts, raisins, sugar, water, and green cardamom. Another notable sweet dish of Bihar is _[balushahi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Balushahi "Balushahi")_ , which is prepared by a specially treated combination of _maida_ and sugar along with _ghee_ , and the other worldwide famous sweet, _[khaja](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Khaja "Khaja")_ is made from flour, vegetable fat, and sugar, which is mainly used in weddings and other occasions. [Silao](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silao,_Nalanda "Silao, Nalanda") near [Nalanda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nalanda "Nalanda") is famous for its production. 
During the festival of [Chhath](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chhath "Chhath"), _[thekua](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thekua "Thekua")_ , a sweet dish made of _ghee_ , [jaggery](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jaggery "Jaggery"), and whole-meal flour, flavoured with [aniseed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aniseed "Aniseed"), is made.[[63]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-HughesMookherjee2001-63)
Other food items that are quite prominent in Bihar are, Pittha, Aaloo Bhujiya, Reshmi [Kebab](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kebab "Kebab"), Palwal ki mithai, and Puri Sabzi.[[67]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-67)
### Chandigarh
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Aloo_Paratha_with_Butter_from_India.jpg/250px-Aloo_Paratha_with_Butter_from_India.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Aloo_Paratha_with_Butter_from_India.jpg)[Punjabi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Punjabi_cuisine "Punjabi cuisine") _aloo paratha_ served with butter
[Chandigarh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chandigarh "Chandigarh"), the capital of [Punjab](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India") and [Haryana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haryana "Haryana") is a city of 20th-century origin with a cosmopolitan food culture mainly involving North Indian cuisine. People enjoy home-made recipes such as _[paratha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paratha "Paratha")_ , especially at breakfast, and other [Punjabi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Punjabi_cuisine "Punjabi cuisine") foods like _[roti](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roti "Roti")_ which is made from [wheat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wheat "Wheat"), [sweetcorn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maize "Maize"), or other [glutenous](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glutenous "Glutenous") [flour](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flour "Flour") with cooked vegetables or beans. _[Sarson da saag](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sarson_da_saag "Sarson da saag")_ and _[dal makhani](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dal_makhani "Dal makhani")_ are well-known dishes among others.[[68]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-68) Popular snacks include _[gol gappa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Panipuri "Panipuri")_ (known as _panipuri_ in other places). It consists of a round, hollow _[puri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Puri_\(food\) "Puri \(food\)")_ , fried crisp and filled with a mixture of flavoured water, boiled and cubed potatoes, [bengal gram](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bengal_gram "Bengal gram") beans, etc. 
### Chhattisgarh
Main article: [Chhattisgarhi cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chhattisgarhi_cuisine "Chhattisgarhi cuisine")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Khurmi%2C_Chhattisgarhi_Cuisine.jpg/250px-Khurmi%2C_Chhattisgarhi_Cuisine.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Khurmi,_Chhattisgarhi_Cuisine.jpg)Chhattisgarhi Sweets Khurmi
[Chhattisgarh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chhattisgarh "Chhattisgarh") cuisine is unique in nature and not found in the rest of India, although the staple food is rice, like in much of the country. Many Chhattisgarhi people drink [liquor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liquor "Liquor") brewed from the [mahuwa flower](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Madhuca_longifolia "Madhuca longifolia") palm wine (_tadi_ in rural areas).[[69]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-69) Chhattisgarhi cuisines varies as per special occasions and festivals like Thethari and Khurmi, fara, gulgule bhajiya, chausela, chila, aaersa are prepared in regional festivals.[[70]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-70) The tribal people of the [Bastar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bastar_district "Bastar district") region of Chhattisgarh eat ancestral dishes such as [mushrooms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edible_mushroom "Edible mushroom"), bamboo pickle, bamboo vegetables, etc.[[71]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Kapoor2002B-71)[[72]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-72)
### Dadra and Nagar Haveli
The local cuisine resembles the cuisine of Gujarat. _[Ubadiyu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Umbadiyu "Umbadiyu")_[[73]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-73) is a local delicacy made of vegetables and beans with herbs. The common foods include rice, _roti_ , vegetables, river fish, and crab. People also enjoy buttermilk and [chutney](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chutney "Chutney") made of different fruits and herbs.[[74]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-74)
### Daman and Diu
[Daman and Diu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daman_and_Diu "Daman and Diu") is a union territory of India which, like [Goa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goa "Goa"), was a former colonial possession of Portugal. Consequently, both native [Gujarati food](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gujarati_cuisine "Gujarati cuisine") and traditional [Portuguese food](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portuguese_cuisine "Portuguese cuisine") are common. Being a coastal region, the communities are mainly dependent on seafood. Normally, _rotli_ and tea are taken for breakfast, _rotla_ and _saak_ for lunch, and _chokha_ along with _saak_ and curry are taken for dinner. Some of the dishes prepared on festive occasions include _puri_ , _lapsee_ , _potaya_ , _dudh-plag_ , and _dhakanu_.[[75]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Singh1994-75) While [alcohol](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alcoholic_beverage "Alcoholic beverage") is prohibited in the neighbouring state of [Gujarat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat"), drinking is common in Daman and Diu. Better known as the "pub" of Gujarat. All popular brands of alcohol are readily available. 
### Delhi
Main article: [Mughlai cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mughlai_cuisine "Mughlai cuisine")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Rajma_Chawal%2C_from_India.jpg/250px-Rajma_Chawal%2C_from_India.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Rajma_Chawal,_from_India.jpg)_[Rajma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajma "Rajma")-[chawal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chawal "Chawal")_, curried red kidney beans with steamed rice[_[why?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")_]
[Delhi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delhi "Delhi") was once the capital of the Mughal empire, and it became the birthplace of [Mughlai cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mughlai_cuisine "Mughlai cuisine"). Delhi is noted for its street food. The [Paranthewali Gali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gali_Paranthe_Wali "Gali Paranthe Wali") in [Chandani Chowk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chandni_Chowk "Chandni Chowk") is just one of the culinary landmarks for stuffed [flatbread](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flatbread "Flatbread") ([_parathas_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paratha#Plain_and_stuffed_varieties "Paratha")). 
Delhi has people from different parts of India, thus the city has different types of food traditions; its cuisine is influenced by the various cultures. Punjabi cuisine is common, due to the dominance of Punjabi communities.[[76]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-76)
Delhi cuisine is actually an amalgam of different Indian cuisines modified in unique ways. This is apparent in the different types of street food available. _Kababs_ , _kachauri_ , _chaat_ , Indian sweets, Indian ice cream (commonly called _[kulfi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kulfi "Kulfi")_), and even Western food items like sandwiches and patties, are prepared in a style unique to Delhi and are quite popular.[[77]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-77)
### Goa
Main articles: [Goan cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goan_cuisine "Goan cuisine") and [Goan Catholic cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goan_Catholic_cuisine "Goan Catholic cuisine")
See also: [Saraswat cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saraswat_cuisine "Saraswat cuisine") and [Malvani cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malvani_cuisine "Malvani cuisine")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Vindalho.jpg/250px-Vindalho.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Vindalho.jpg)Pork _[vindaloo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vindaloo "Vindaloo")_ (pictured) is a popular curry dish in Goa and around the world.
The area has a tropical climate, which means the spices and flavours are intense. Use of _[kokum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Garcinia_indica "Garcinia indica")_ is a distinct feature of the region's cuisine. 
Goan cuisine is mostly seafood and meat-based; the staple foods are rice and fish. [Kingfish (_vison_ or _visvan_)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indo-Pacific_king_mackerel "Indo-Pacific king mackerel") is the most common delicacy, and others include [pomfret](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pomfret "Pomfret"), [shark](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shark_meat "Shark meat"), [tuna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tuna "Tuna"), and [mackerel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mackerel_\(food\) "Mackerel \(food\)"); these are often served with [coconut milk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coconut_milk "Coconut milk").[[78]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Chapman2009-78) [Shellfish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shellfish "Shellfish"), including [crabs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crab_meat "Crab meat"), [prawns](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shrimp_and_prawn_as_food "Shrimp and prawn as food"), [tiger prawns](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tiger_prawn "Tiger prawn"), [lobster](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lobster_meat "Lobster meat"), [squid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Squid_\(food\) "Squid \(food\)"), and [mussels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mussel#As_food "Mussel"), are commonly eaten. 
The cuisine of Goa is influenced by its Hindu origins, 400 years of [Portuguese colonialism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"), and modern techniques.[[78]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Chapman2009-78)[[79]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-ThomasKarafin2009-79)
[Bread](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bread "Bread"), introduced by the Portuguese, is very popular, and is an important part of the Goan breakfast, most frequently in the form of toast. 
### Gujarat
Main article: [Gujarati cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gujarati_cuisine "Gujarati cuisine")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Khaman.jpg/250px-Khaman.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Khaman.jpg)_[Khaman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Khaman "Khaman")_ is a popular Gujarati snack
Gujarati cuisine is primarily vegetarian. The typical Gujarati _[thali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thali "Thali")_ consists of _roti_ (_rotlii_ in [Gujarati](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language")), _daal_ or _[kadhi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kadhi "Kadhi")_ , rice, _sabzi_ /_shaak_ , _[papad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Papadum "Papadum")_ and _[chaas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chaas "Chaas")_ (buttermilk). 
_Sabzi_ is a dish of different combinations of vegetables and spices which may be [stir fried](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stir_frying "Stir frying"), spicy or sweet.[[80]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-80) Gujarati cuisine can vary widely in flavour and heat based on personal and regional tastes. [North Gujarat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_Gujarat "North Gujarat"), [Kathiawad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kathiawad "Kathiawad"), [Kachchh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kachchh "Kachchh"), and [South Gujarat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Gujarat "South Gujarat") are the four major regions of Gujarati cuisine.[[81]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Gujurati_cuisine_regions-81)
Many Gujarati dishes are simultaneously sweet, salty (like _[handvo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Handvo "Handvo")_), and spicy. In [mango](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mango "Mango") season, _keri no ras_ (fresh mango pulp) is often an integral part of the meal. Spices also vary seasonally. For example, _[garam masala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Garam_masala "Garam masala")_ is used much less in summer. 
Gujarati snacks include _sev khamani_ ,[[82]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-82) _[khakhra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Khakhra "Khakhra")_ , _dal vada_ ,[[83]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-83) _methi na bhajiya_ ,[[84]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-84) _[khaman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Khaman "Khaman")_ , _[bhakharwadi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bakarwadi "Bakarwadi")_ and more. 
Regular [fasting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fasting "Fasting"), with diets limited to milk, [dried fruit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dried_fruit "Dried fruit"), and [nuts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nut_\(fruit\) "Nut \(fruit\)"), is a common practice,[[85]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-ThakerBarton2012-85)
### Haryana
Main article: [Cuisine of Haryana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cuisine_of_Haryana "Cuisine of Haryana")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Kadhi_Chawal_from_India.jpg/250px-Kadhi_Chawal_from_India.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Kadhi_Chawal_from_India.jpg)_Kadhi_ is a [Haryana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haryana "Haryana") dish.
Cattle being common in [Haryana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haryana "Haryana"), dairy products are a common component of its cuisine.[[86]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-LimitedKitchen2010-86)[[87]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-WickramasingheRajah2005-87)
Specific regional dishes include _[kadhi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kadhi#India "Kadhi")_ , _[pakora](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pakora "Pakora")_ , _[besan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gram_flour "Gram flour") [masala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spice_mix "Spice mix") roti_,[[88]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-88) _bajra aloo roti_ ,[[89]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-89) _[churma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Churma "Churma")_ , _[kheer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kheer "Kheer")_ , _bathua raita_ ,[[90]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-90) _methi[gajar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carrot "Carrot")_,[[91]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-91) _singri ki[sabzi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curry "Curry")_,[[92]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-92) and [tomato chutney](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tomato_chutney "Tomato chutney"). 
In the past, its staple diet included _bajra khichdi_ ,[[93]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-93) _[rabdi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rabri "Rabri")_ , onion chutney,[[94]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-94) and _bajra ki roti_.[[95]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-95) In non-vegetarian cuisine it includes _kukad kadhai_[[96]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-96) and [chicken _tikka masala_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicken_tikka_masala "Chicken tikka masala"). 
_[Lassi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lassi "Lassi")_ , _[sharbat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sharbat_\(beverage\) "Sharbat \(beverage\)")_ , _[nimbu pani](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lemonade "Lemonade")_ and _[labsi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laapsi "Laapsi")_ (a mixture of _bajra_ flour and _lassi_) are three popular non-alcoholic beverages in Haryana. However, liquor stores are common there, which cater to a large number of truck drivers.[[97]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-97)
### Himachal Pradesh
Main article: [Culture of Himachal Pradesh § Cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Culture_of_Himachal_Pradesh#Cuisine "Culture of Himachal Pradesh")
The daily diet of [Himachal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Himachal "Himachal") people is similar to that of the rest of North India, including lentils, broth, rice, vegetables, and bread, although non-vegetarian cuisine is preferred. Some of the specialities of Himachal include _sidu_ ,[[98]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-98) _patande_ ,[[99]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-99) _[chukh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chamba_Chukh "Chamba Chukh")_ , _[rajmah](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajma "Rajma")_ , and _[til](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sesame "Sesame")_ chutney.[[100]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Himachal_cuisine-100)
### Jammu and Kashmir
Main article: [Kashmiri cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kashmiri_cuisine "Kashmiri cuisine")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Kashmiri_cuisine_waazwan.jpg/250px-Kashmiri_cuisine_waazwan.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Kashmiri_cuisine_waazwan.jpg)[Wazwan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wazwan "Wazwan")
The cuisine of Jammu and Kashmir is from two regions of the state: Jammu division and Kashmir Valley. [Kashmiri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir") cuisine has evolved over hundreds of years. Its first major influence was the food of the [Kashmiri Hindus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kashmiri_Pandits "Kashmiri Pandits") and Buddhists. 
The cuisine was later influenced by the cultures which arrived with the invasion of Kashmir by [Timur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Timur "Timur") from the area of modern [Uzbekistan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"). Subsequent influences have included the cuisines of [Central Asia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") and the North Indian plains. 
The most notable ingredient in Kashmiri cuisine is [mutton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lamb_and_mutton#Indian_subcontinent "Lamb and mutton"), of which over 30 varieties are known.[[101]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Kashmiri_cuisine-101) _[Wazwan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wazwan "Wazwan")_ is a multicourse meal in the Kashmiri tradition, the preparation of which is considered an art.[[102]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Wazwan-102)
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Shufta_Kashmiri_cuisine.jpg/250px-Shufta_Kashmiri_cuisine.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Shufta_Kashmiri_cuisine.jpg)Shufta
Kashmiri pandit food is elaborate, and an important part of the Pandits' ethnic identity. Kashmiri pandit cuisine usually uses _[dahi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dahi_\(curd\) "Dahi \(curd\)")_ (yogurt), oil, and spices such as turmeric, red chilli, cumin, ginger, and [fennel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fennel "Fennel"), though they do not use onion and garlic.[[103]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-103) _[Birayanis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biryani "Biryani")_ are quite popular, and are the speciality of Kashmir. 
The Jammu region is famous for its _[sund panjeeri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Panjiri "Panjiri")_ , [_patisa_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soan_papdi "Soan papdi"), _[rajma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rajma "Rajma")_ with rice and [Kalari cheese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kalari_cheese "Kalari cheese"). 
[Dogri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dogri_language "Dogri language") food includes _ambal_ (sour pumpkin dish),[[104]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-104) _khatta_ meat,[[105]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-105) _[kulthein di dal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Macrotyloma_uniflorum "Macrotyloma uniflorum")_ ,[[106]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-106) _dal chawal_ ,[[107]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-107) _maa da madra_ (black gram lentils in yogurt)[[108]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-108) and Uriya. 
Many types of pickles are made including [mango](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mango_pickle "Mango pickle"), _[kasrod](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fiddlehead#Indian_cuisine "Fiddlehead")_ , and _girgle_. Street food is also famous which include various types of _[chaats](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chaat "Chaat")_ , specially [_gol gappas_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Panipuri "Panipuri"), _[gulgule](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gulgula_\(doughnut\) "Gulgula \(doughnut\)")_ , _[chole bhature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chole_bhature "Chole bhature")_ , _rajma kulcha_[[109]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-109) and [_dahi bhalla_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dahi_vada "Dahi vada"). 
### Jharkhand
Main article: [Cuisine of Jharkhand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cuisine_of_Jharkhand "Cuisine of Jharkhand")
Staple foods in [Jharkhand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jharkhand "Jharkhand") are rice, _dal_ and vegetables. Famous dishes include _chirka roti_ ,[[110]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-110) _[pittha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pitha "Pitha")_ , _[malpua](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malpua "Malpua")_ , _[dhuska](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dhooska "Dhooska")_ , _arsa roti_[[111]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-111) and _[litti chokha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Litti_\(cuisine\) "Litti \(cuisine\)")_.[[112]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-112)
Local alcoholic drinks include _[handia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Handia_\(drink\) "Handia \(drink\)")_ , a rice beer, and _mahua[daru](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desi_daru "Desi daru")_, made from flowers of the _mahua_ tree (_[Madhuca longifolia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Madhuca_longifolia "Madhuca longifolia"))_.[[113]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-113)[[114]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-114)
### Karnataka
Main article: [Cuisine of Karnataka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cuisine_of_Karnataka "Cuisine of Karnataka")
See also: [Mangalorean Catholic cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mangalorean_Catholic_cuisine "Mangalorean Catholic cuisine") and [Udupi cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Udupi_cuisine "Udupi cuisine")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Another_Vegetarian_Meal.jpg/250px-Another_Vegetarian_Meal.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Another_Vegetarian_Meal.jpg)Staple vegetarian meal of Karnataka is _[jolada rotti](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jolada_rotti "Jolada rotti"), palya_, and _anna-saaru_.
A number of dishes, such as _[idli](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Idli "Idli"), [rava idli](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rava_idli "Rava idli")_, Mysore _[masala dosa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Masala_dosa "Masala dosa")_ , etc., were invented here and have become popular beyond the state of [Karnataka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka")[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]. Equally, varieties in the cuisine of Karnataka have similarities with its three neighbouring South Indian states, as well as the states of [Maharashtra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra") and Goa to its north. It is very common for the food to be served on a banana leaf, especially during festivals and functions. 
Karnataka cuisine can be very broadly divided into [Mysore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mysore "Mysore")/[Bangalore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Culture_of_Bangalore#Cuisine "Culture of Bangalore") cuisine, North Karnataka cuisine, [Udupi cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Udupi_cuisine "Udupi cuisine"), [Kodagu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kodagu "Kodagu")/Coorg cuisine, [Karavali](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karavali "Karavali")/coastal cuisine, and [Saraswat cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saraswat_cuisine "Saraswat cuisine"). [Catholic Christians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catholic_Christian "Catholic Christian") from [Mangalore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mangalore "Mangalore") have their distinct cuisine featuring coconut, spices, and a distinct Portuguese influence. 
This cuisine covers a wide spectrum of food from pure vegetarian and vegan to meats like pork, and from savouries to sweets. 
Typical dishes include _[bisi bele bath](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bisi_bele_bath "Bisi bele bath")_ , _[jolada rotti](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jolada_rotti "Jolada rotti")_ , _badanekai yennegai_ ,[[115]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-115) _[holige](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Holige "Holige")_ , _kadubu_ , _[chapati](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chapati "Chapati")_ , _[idli vada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Idli "Idli")_ , _[ragi rotti](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ragi_rotti "Ragi rotti")_ , _[akki rotti](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Akki_rotti "Akki rotti")_ , _[saaru](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rasam_\(dish\) "Rasam \(dish\)")_ , _[huli](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sambar_\(dish\) "Sambar \(dish\)")_ , _[kootu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kootu "Kootu")_ , _[vangibath](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vangibath "Vangibath")_ , _[khara bath](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kiribath "Kiribath")_ , _[kesari bhath](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kesari_bhath "Kesari bhath")_ , _[sajjige](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sajjige "Sajjige")_ , _[neer dosa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neer_dosa "Neer dosa"),_ _mysoore_[_[clarification needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")_], _haal bai_ ,[[116]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-116) _[chiroti](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chiroti "Chiroti")_ , _[benne dose](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Benne_dose "Benne dose")_ , [majjige huli](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Majjige_huli "Majjige huli"), _[ragi mudde](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ragi_mudde "Ragi mudde"),_ and _[uppittu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uppittu "Uppittu")._
The [Kodagu district](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kodagu_district "Kodagu district") is known for spicy pork curries,[[117]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-117) while coastal Karnataka specialises in seafood. Although the ingredients differ regionally, a typical _Kannadiga oota_ (Kannadiga meal) is served on a [banana leaf](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banana_leaf "Banana leaf"). The coastal districts of [Dakshina Kannada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dakshina_Kannada "Dakshina Kannada") and [Udupi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Udupi_district "Udupi district") have slightly varying cuisines, which make extensive use of coconut in curries and frequently include seafood.[[118]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-118)[[119]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Ranade2009-119)
### Kerala
Main article: [Cuisine of Kerala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cuisine_of_Kerala "Cuisine of Kerala")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/South_Indian_food_cuisine.jpg/250px-South_Indian_food_cuisine.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:South_Indian_food_cuisine.jpg)A traditional Kerala Sadhya
