## Hindu fasting cuisine
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Sabudana_Khichdi.jpg/250px-Sabudana_Khichdi.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Sabudana_Khichdi.jpg)_Sabudana khichadi_ , a snack, popular on Hindu fasting days
Hindu people fast on days such as [Ekadashi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ekadashi "Ekadashi"), in honour of Lord [Vishnu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu") or his [Avatars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Avatar "Avatar"), [Chaturthi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chaturthi "Chaturthi") in honour of [Ganesh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganesh "Ganesh"), Mondays in honour of [Shiva](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shiva "Shiva"), or Saturdays in honour of [Hanuman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanuman "Hanuman") or [Saturn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saturn "Saturn").[[207]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal20106-207)
Only certain kinds of foods are allowed to be eaten. These include milk and other dairy products (such as [_dahi_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dahi_\(curd\) "Dahi \(curd\)")), fruit, and Western food items such as _[sago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sago "Sago")_ ,[[208]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-208) [potatoes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Potato "Potato"),[[209]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-209) purple-red [sweet potatoes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sweet_potato "Sweet potato"), [amaranth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amaranth "Amaranth") seeds,[[210]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-210) [nuts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nut_\(fruit\) "Nut \(fruit\)") and ([shama millet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shama_millet "Shama millet")).[[211]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal20107-211)
Popular fasting dishes include _farari chevdo_ ,[[212]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-212) _[sabudana khichadi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sabudana_Khichadi "Sabudana Khichadi")_ , and [peanut soup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peanut_soup "Peanut soup").[[213]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalal201063-213)
## Diaspora and fusion cuisines
The interaction of various [Indian diaspora](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-resident_Indian_and_person_of_Indian_origin "Non-resident Indian and person of Indian origin") communities with the native cultures in their new homes has resulted in the creation of many [fusion cuisines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fusion_cuisine "Fusion cuisine"), which blend aspects of Indian and other international cuisines. These cuisines tend to interpolate Indian seasoning and cooking techniques into their own national dishes. 
### Indian Chinese cuisine
Main article: [Indian Chinese cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Chinese_cuisine "Indian Chinese cuisine")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Chicken_Manchurian_%28Hyderabad_Style%29_%2811960049916%29.jpg/250px-Chicken_Manchurian_%28Hyderabad_Style%29_%2811960049916%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Chicken_Manchurian_\(Hyderabad_Style\)_\(11960049916\).jpg)Chicken Manchurian served in [Hyderabad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hyderabad "Hyderabad")
Indian Chinese cuisine, also known as Indo-Chinese cuisine originated in the 19th century among the [Chinese community of Calcutta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_in_India "Chinese in India"), during the immigration of [Hakka Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese") from [Canton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") (present-day Guangzhou) seeking to escape the [First](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") and [Second Opium Wars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_Opium_War "Second Opium War") and political instability in the region.[[214]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Mannur-214) Upon exposure to local Indian cuisine, they incorporated many spices and cooking techniques into their [own cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hakka_cuisine "Hakka cuisine"), thus creating a unique fusion of Indian and Chinese cuisine.[[214]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Mannur-214)
After 1947, many [Cantonese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese_people "Cantonese people") immigrants opened restaurants in [Calcutta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta"), serving dishes that combined aspects of Indian cuisine with [Cantonese cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese_cuisine "Cantonese cuisine").[[215]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Banerjee-215) In other parts of India, Indian Chinese cuisine is derived from [Calcutta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta")-[Chinese cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine"), but bears little resemblance to their Chinese counterparts[[215]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Banerjee-215) as the dishes tend to be flavoured with [cumin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cumin "Cumin"), [coriander](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coriander "Coriander") seeds, and [turmeric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turmeric "Turmeric"), which with a few regional exceptions, are not traditionally associated with Chinese cuisine.[[216]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Deshpande-216) [Chilli](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chili_pepper "Chili pepper"), [ginger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ginger "Ginger"), [garlic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Garlic "Garlic") and [_dahi_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dahi_\(curd\) "Dahi \(curd\)") (yogurt) are also frequently used in dishes.[[216]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Deshpande-216)
Popular dishes include [Chicken Manchurian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicken_Manchurian "Chicken Manchurian"), [chicken lollipop](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicken_lollipop "Chicken lollipop"), [chilli chicken](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chilli_chicken "Chilli chicken"), Hakka noodles, Hunan chicken, [chow mein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chow_mein "Chow mein"), and Szechwan [fried rice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fried_rice "Fried rice"). 
Soups such as [Manchow soup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manchow_soup "Manchow soup") and [sweet corn soup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corn_soup "Corn soup") are popular, whereas desserts include ice cream on honey-fried noodles and date pancakes.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]
### Indian Thai cuisine
Thai cuisine was [influenced](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_Indian_influence_on_Southeast_Asia#Thailand "History of Indian influence on Southeast Asia") by Indian cuisine, like as recorded by the Thai monk [Buddhadasa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buddhadasa "Buddhadasa") Bhikku in his writing 'India's Benevolence to Thailand'. He wrote that Thai people learned how to use [spices](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ayurveda "Ayurveda") in their food in various ways from Indians. Thais also obtained the methods of making [herbal medicines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ayurveda "Ayurveda") (Ayurveda) from the Indians. Some plants like sarabhi of family [Guttiferae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clusiaceae "Clusiaceae"), kanika or [harsinghar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parijata "Parijata"), phikun or _[Mimusops elengi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mimusops_elengi "Mimusops elengi")_ and bunnak or the [rose chestnut](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mesua_ferrea "Mesua ferrea") etc. were brought from India. 
### Malaysian Indian cuisine
Main article: [Malaysian Indian cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malaysian_Indian_cuisine "Malaysian Indian cuisine")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Indian_rojak.JPG/250px-Indian_rojak.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Indian_rojak.JPG)Indian _rojak_ in Malaysia
Malaysian Indian cuisine, or the cooking of the ethnic Indian communities in Malaysia consists of adaptations of authentic dishes from India, as well as original creations inspired by the diverse food culture of Malaysia. 
A typical Malaysian Indian dish is likely to be redolent with curry leaves, whole and powdered spice, and contains fresh coconut in various forms. 
_Ghee_ is still widely used for cooking, although vegetable oils and refined palm oils are now commonplace in home kitchens. 
### Indian Singaporean cuisine
Main article: [Indian Singaporean cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Singaporean_cuisine "Indian Singaporean cuisine")
Indian Singaporean cuisine refers to foods and beverages produced and [consumed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singaporean_cuisine "Singaporean cuisine") in [Singapore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") that are derived, wholly or in part, from South Asian culinary traditions. 
The great variety of Singaporean food includes Indian food, which tends to be Tamil cuisine, especially local Tamil Muslim cuisine, although North Indian food[[217]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-217) has become more visible recently. 
Indian dishes have become modified to different degrees, after years of contact with other Singaporean cultures, and in response to locally available ingredients, as well as changing local tastes. 
### Indian Indonesian cuisine
Main article: [Indian Indonesian cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_Indonesian_cuisine "Indian Indonesian cuisine")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Roti_Cane_Kari_Kambing_Aceh.jpg/250px-Roti_Cane_Kari_Kambing_Aceh.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Roti_Cane_Kari_Kambing_Aceh.jpg)_[Roti canai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roti_canai "Roti canai")_ and [mutton curry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mutton_curry "Mutton curry"), Indian influence on [Indonesian cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesian_cuisine "Indonesian cuisine")
Indian-Indonesian cuisine refers to food and beverages in Indonesian cuisine that have influenced Indian cuisine—especially from [Tamil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tamil_cuisine "Tamil cuisine"), [Punjabi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Punjabi_cuisine "Punjabi cuisine"), and [Gujarati cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gujarati_cuisine "Gujarati cuisine"). These dishes are well integrated, such as _[appam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Appam "Appam")_ , _[biryani](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biryani "Biryani")_ , _[murtabak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Murtabak "Murtabak")_ and [curry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curry "Curry"). 
### Indian Filipino cuisine
[Filipino cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Filipino_cuisine "Filipino cuisine"), found throughout the [Philippines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") archipelago, has been [historically influenced by the Indian cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_the_Philippines#Indianised "History of the Philippines"). Indian influences can also be noted in rice-based delicacies such as _[bibingka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bibingka "Bibingka")_ (analogous to the Indonesian _[bingka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bibingka#Bibingka_in_Indonesia "Bibingka")_), _[puto](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Puto_\(food\) "Puto \(food\)")_ , and _[puto bumbong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Puto_bumbong "Puto bumbong")_ , where the latter two are plausibly derived from the [south Indian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Indian_cuisine "South Indian cuisine") _[puttu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Puttu "Puttu")_ , which also has variants throughout [Maritime Southeast Asia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maritime_Southeast_Asia "Maritime Southeast Asia") (e.g. _[kue putu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kue_putu "Kue putu")_ , _[putu mangkok](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Putu_mangkok "Putu mangkok")_). 
The _[kare-kare](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kare-kare "Kare-kare")_ , more popular in Luzon, on the other hand could trace its origins from the [Seven Years' War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") when the [British occupied Manila](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_occupation_of_Manila "British occupation of Manila") from 1762 to 1764 with a force that included Indian [sepoys](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sepoy "Sepoy"), who had to improvise Indian dishes given the lack of spices in the Philippines to make [curry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curry "Curry"). This is said to explain the name and its supposed thick, yellow-to-orange [annatto](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Annatto "Annatto") and peanut-based sauce, which alludes to a type of curry. 
[Atchara](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atchara "Atchara") of Philippines originated from the Indian _[achar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Asian_pickle "South Asian pickle")_ , which was transmitted to the Philippines via the [acar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acar "Acar") of the [Indonesia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), [Malaysia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), and [Brunei](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"). 
### Anglo-Indian cuisine
Main article: [Anglo-Indian cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anglo-Indian_cuisine "Anglo-Indian cuisine")
[Anglo-Indian cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anglo-Indian_cuisine "Anglo-Indian cuisine") developed during the period of [British colonial rule](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") in India, as British officials interacted with their Indian cooks.[[218]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Davidson-218)
Well-known Anglo-Indian dishes include [chutneys](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chutney "Chutney"), salted [beef tongue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beef_tongue "Beef tongue"), [kedgeree](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kedgeree "Kedgeree"),[[219]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-219) ball curry, fish [rissoles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rissole "Rissole"), and [mulligatawny soup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mulligatawny_soup "Mulligatawny soup").[[218]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Davidson-218)[[220]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-220)[[221]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-221)
## Desserts
Main article: [List of Indian sweets and desserts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Indian_sweets_and_desserts "List of Indian sweets and desserts")
[![Kheer](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Kheer.jpg/250px-Kheer.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kheer "Kheer")
Kheer
[![Phirni](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Phirni.jpg/250px-Phirni.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phirni "Phirni")
Phirni
_[Phirni](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phirni "Phirni")_ and _[kheer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kheer "Kheer")_ are two of the most popular [rice puddings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rice_pudding "Rice pudding") in India.
Many Indian desserts, or _[mithai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mithai_\(confectionery\) "Mithai \(confectionery\)")_ , are fried foods made with sugar, milk or [condensed milk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Condensed_milk "Condensed milk"). Ingredients and preferred types of dessert vary by region. In the eastern part of India, for example, most are based on milk products. 
Many are flavoured with almonds and pistachios, spiced with cardamon, nutmeg, cloves and black pepper, and decorated with nuts, or with gold or silver leaf. Popular Indian desserts include _[rasogolla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rasogolla "Rasogolla")_ , _[gulab jamun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gulab_jamun "Gulab jamun")_ , _[jalebi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jalebi "Jalebi")_ , _[laddu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laddu "Laddu")_ , and _[peda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peda "Peda")_.[[222]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-222)
## Beverages
See also: [List of Indian drinks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Indian_drinks "List of Indian drinks")
### Non-alcoholic beverages
[Tea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tea "Tea") is a staple beverage throughout India, since the country is one of the largest producers of tea in the world. The most popular varieties of tea grown in India include [Assam tea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assam_tea "Assam tea"), [Darjeeling tea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darjeeling_tea "Darjeeling tea") and [Nilgiri tea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nilgiri_tea "Nilgiri tea"). It is prepared by boiling the tea leaves in a mix of water, milk, and spices such as cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and ginger. In India, tea is often enjoyed with snacks like [biscuits](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biscuit "Biscuit") and _[pakoda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pakora "Pakora")_.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]
[Coffee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coffee "Coffee") is another popular beverage, but more popular in South India.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_] Coffee is also cultivated in some parts of India. There are two varieties of coffee popular in India, which include [Indian filter coffee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_filter_coffee "Indian filter coffee") and [instant coffee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Instant_coffee "Instant coffee").[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]
_[Lassi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lassi "Lassi")_ is a traditional [_dahi_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dahi_\(curd\) "Dahi \(curd\)") (yogurt)-based drink in India.[[223]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-BalasubramanianJannu2011-223) It is made by blending yogurt with water or milk and spices. Salted _lassi_ is more common in villages of Punjab and in [Porbandar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Porbandar "Porbandar"), Gujarat.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_] Traditional _lassi_ is sometimes flavoured with ground roasted cumin. _Lassi_ can also be flavoured with ingredients such as sugar, rose water, [mango](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mango "Mango"), [lemon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lemon "Lemon"), [strawberry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strawberry "Strawberry"), and saffron.[[224]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-BrownThomas2008-224)
_[Sharbat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sharbat_\(beverage\) "Sharbat \(beverage\)")_ is a sweet cold beverage prepared from fruits or flower petals.[[225]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-HerbstHerbst2007-225) It can be served in concentrate form and eaten with a spoon, or diluted with water to create a drink. Popular _sharbats_ are made from plants such as [rose](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rose "Rose"), [sandalwood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sandalwood "Sandalwood"), _bel_ , _gurhal_ ([hibiscus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hibiscus "Hibiscus")), lemon, [orange](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orange_\(fruit\) "Orange \(fruit\)"), [pineapple](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pineapple "Pineapple"), [_sarasaparilla_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hemidesmus_indicus "Hemidesmus indicus") and _kokum_ , _falsa_ (_[Grewia asiatica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grewia_asiatica "Grewia asiatica")_). In [Ayurveda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ayurveda "Ayurveda"), _sharbats_ are believed to hold medicinal value.[[226]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-226)
_[Thandai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thandai "Thandai")_ is a cold drink prepared with a mixture of [almonds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Almond "Almond"), [fennel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fennel "Fennel") seeds, [watermelon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Watermelon "Watermelon") kernels, [rose](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rose "Rose") petals, [pepper](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Black_pepper "Black pepper"), [poppy seeds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum"), [cardamom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cardamom "Cardamom"), [saffron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saffron "Saffron"), [milk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milk "Milk") and [sugar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sugar "Sugar"). It is native to [India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/India "India") and is often associated with the [Maha Shivaratri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maha_Shivaratri "Maha Shivaratri") and [Holi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Holi "Holi") or [Holla mahalla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hola_Mohalla "Hola Mohalla") festival. Sometimes _[bhaang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bhang "Bhang")_ (cannabis) is added to prepare special thandai. 
Other beverages include _[nimbu pani](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lemonade "Lemonade")_ (lemonade), _[chaas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chaas "Chaas")_ , _badam doodh_ (milk with nuts – mostly almonds – and cardamom), _[Aam panna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aam_panna "Aam panna")_ , _[kokum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kokum "Kokum") sharbat_, and coconut water. 
Modern carbonated cold drinks unique to southern India include beverages, such as _panner soda_ or _goli soda_ , a mixture of [carbonated water](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbonated_water "Carbonated water"), rose water, rose milk, and sugar, _naranga soda_ , a mixture of [carbonated water](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbonated_water "Carbonated water"), salt and lemon juice, and _nannari sarbath_ , a mixture with [_sarasaparilla_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hemidesmus_indicus "Hemidesmus indicus"). 
_Sharbats_ with carbonated water are the most popular non-alcoholic beverages in Kerala and [Tamil Nadu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu"). Street shops in Central Kerala and Madurai region of Tamil Nadu are well known for these drinks which are also called _kulukki sarbaths_ in Kerala.
  * [![Darjeeling tea in varieties.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Darjeeling%2C_India%2C_Darjeeling_tea_in_variety%2C_Black_tea.jpg/250px-Darjeeling%2C_India%2C_Darjeeling_tea_in_variety%2C_Black_tea.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Darjeeling,_India,_Darjeeling_tea_in_variety,_Black_tea.jpg "Darjeeling tea in varieties.")
[Darjeeling tea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darjeeling_tea "Darjeeling tea") in varieties.
  * [![Indian filter coffee is popular in Southern India.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Filter_Kaapi_at_Sarvana_Bhavan_Restaurant%2C_Chennai%2C_Tamil_Nadu.jpg/120px-Filter_Kaapi_at_Sarvana_Bhavan_Restaurant%2C_Chennai%2C_Tamil_Nadu.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Filter_Kaapi_at_Sarvana_Bhavan_Restaurant,_Chennai,_Tamil_Nadu.jpg "Indian filter coffee is popular in Southern India.")
[Indian filter coffee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_filter_coffee "Indian filter coffee") is popular in Southern India.
  * [![Badam milk](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/BadamMilk.JPG/250px-BadamMilk.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:BadamMilk.JPG "Badam milk")
[_Badam_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Almond "Almond") milk

Holi Special Chilled [Thandai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thandai "Thandai")


### Alcoholic beverages
#### Beer
Main article: [Beer in India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beer_in_India "Beer in India")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Bastar_Beer.jpg/250px-Bastar_Beer.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Bastar_Beer.jpg)Bastar Beer prepared from Sulfi
Most [beers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beer "Beer") in India are either lagers (4.8 [percent alcohol](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alcohol_by_volume "Alcohol by volume")) or strong lagers (8.9 percent). The Indian beer industry has witnessed steady growth of 10–17 percent per year over the last ten years.[_[when?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")_] Production exceeded 170 million cases during the 2008–2009 financial year.[[227]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-227) With the average age of the population decreasing and income levels on the rise, the popularity of beer in the country continues to increase. 
#### Others
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Chhyang_or_Chhaang_-_Fermented_rice_drink%21_%288902831091%29.jpg/250px-Chhyang_or_Chhaang_-_Fermented_rice_drink%21_%288902831091%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Chhyang_or_Chhaang_-_Fermented_rice_drink!_\(8902831091\).jpg)Nepalese _chhaang_ brewed from rice
Other popular alcoholic drinks in India include _[fenny](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Feni_\(liquor\) "Feni \(liquor\)")_ , a Goan liquor made from either coconut or the juice of the [cashew apple](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cashew_apple "Cashew apple"). The state of Goa has registered for a [geographical indicator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geographical_indication "Geographical indication") to allow its _fenny_ distilleries to claim exclusive rights to production of liquor under the name "_fenny_."[[228]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-228)
_[Hadia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Handia_\(drink\) "Handia \(drink\)")_ is a rice beer, created by mixing herbs with boiled rice and leaving the mixture to ferment for around a week. It is served cold and is less alcoholic than other Indian liquors. _[Chuak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chuak "Chuak")_ is a similar drink from [Tripura](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tripura "Tripura"). 
[Palm wine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palm_wine#South_Asia "Palm wine"), locally known as _[neera](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neera "Neera")_ , is a sap extracted from [inflorescences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inflorescence "Inflorescence") of various species of [toddy palms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cocos_nucifera "Cocos nucifera").[[229]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-All-Asia_guide-229)
_[Chhaang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chhaang "Chhaang")_ is consumed by the people of [Sikkim](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sikkim "Sikkim") and the [Darjeeling Himalayan hill region](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darjeeling_Himalayan_hill_region "Darjeeling Himalayan hill region") of West Bengal. It is drunk cold or at room temperature in summer, and often hot during cold weather. _Chhaang_ is similar to traditional beer, brewed from barley, millet, or rice.[[230]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Bamforth2010-230)
_Kallu_ (_Chetthu Kallu_) is a popular natural alcohol extracted from coconut and pine trees in Kerala. It is sold in local _Kallu_ shops and is consumed with fried fish and chicken. Its alcoholic content is increased by addition of distilled alcohol. 
## Eating habits
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/South_Indian_style_Paan%2C_Dakshin_Sheraton%2C_Bangalore.jpg/250px-South_Indian_style_Paan%2C_Dakshin_Sheraton%2C_Bangalore.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:South_Indian_style_Paan,_Dakshin_Sheraton,_Bangalore.jpg)_[Paan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paan "Paan")_ is often eaten after a meal.
Indians consider a healthy breakfast important. They generally prefer to drink tea or coffee with breakfast, though food preferences vary regionally. North Indian people prefer _[roti](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roti "Roti")_ , _[parathas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paratha "Paratha")_ , and a vegetable dish accompanied by _[achar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_pickle "Indian pickle")_ (a pickle) and some curd.[[231]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Dalal2001\(2\)-231) Various types of packaged pickles are available in the market. One of the oldest pickle-making companies in India is Harnarains,[[232]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-232) which started in the 1860s in [Old Delhi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Old_Delhi "Old Delhi"). 
People of Gujarat prefer _[dhokla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dhokla "Dhokla")_ and milk, while south Indians prefer _idli_ and _dosa_ , generally accompanied by _sambhar_ or _sagu_ and various _[chutneys](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chutney "Chutney")_.[[233]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-BruynBain2010-233)
Traditional lunch in India usually consists of a main dish of rice in the south and the east, and whole-wheat _rotis_ in the north. It typically includes two or three kinds of vegetables, and sometimes items such as _[kulcha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kulcha "Kulcha")_ , _[naan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Naan "Naan")_ , or _[parathas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paratha "Paratha")_. _[Paan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paan "Paan")_ (stuffed, spiced and folded [betel leaves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Betel_leaves "Betel leaves")) which aids digestion is often eaten after lunch and dinner in many parts of India.[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Kapoor2002-38)
Indian families often gather for "evening snack time", similar to [tea time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tea_\(meal\) "Tea \(meal\)") to talk and have tea and snacks. 
Dinner is considered the main meal of the day.[[234]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-KittlerSucher2011-234) Also, many households, especially in north and central India, prefer having sweets after the dinner (similar to the Western concept of dessert after meals). 
## Dietary practices
In India people often follow dietary practices based on their religious beliefs, 
  * Many [Hindu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hinduism_in_India "Hinduism in India") communities consider beef taboo since they believed that Hindu scriptures condemn cow slaughter. Cow slaughter has been banned in many states of India,[[235]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-235) with the exceptions of the North-Eastern states, West Bengal and Kerala. However, buffalo meat is available in some of these states to be consumed by other religious communities.
  * The taboo on beef is defied by the marginalised Hindu castes in the [Dalit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dalit "Dalit") fold,[[236]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-236) substantially covered in their [literature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dalit_literature "Dalit literature") and often displayed at their food festivals.[[237]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-237) As Dalit communities were restricted to living outside the village in mostly barren lands, with reduced resources to cultivate their food, discarded cows were essential traditional food that nourished them. Dalit cuisine included dishes made with coagulated beef blood, preserved meat, and intestines.[[238]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-238) During the independence era, and after, the imposition of beef restrictions on Dalit communities was part of efforts to bring them under a unified nationalist identity.[[239]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-239)
  * [Vaishnavism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vaishnavism "Vaishnavism") followers generally are strict lacto-vegetarians due to an emphasis on [Ahimsa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ahimsa "Ahimsa"). They also do not consume garlic and onions.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]
  * [Jains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jainism_in_India "Jainism in India") follow a strict form of lacto-vegetarianism, known as [Jain vegetarianism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jain_vegetarianism "Jain vegetarianism"), which in addition to being completely lacto-vegetarian, also excludes all root vegetables such as carrots and potatoes because when the root is pulled up, organisms that live around the root also die.[[240]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Shah1998-240)
  * [Muslims](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islam_in_India "Islam in India") do not eat pork or pork products. They consume buffalo and beef products.
  * In certain [North-Eastern regions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Northeast_India "Northeast India"), canines are part of culinary heritage. While only some communities eat dog meat, it is presently neither universal nor widely consumed. Nevertheless, it has been a constant source of stereotype and racial discrimination against people from the region.[[241]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-241)[[242]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-242)


In India dietary practices are also closely related to caste discrimination practices as they are rooted in notions of pollution and filth. Dalit and tribal accounts on cooking and eating narrate casteist-racist treatment accorded to Dalit and tribal culinary practices. This sometimes attract policing on the grounds of creating public nuisance by raising civic concerns. Brahmanical food practices dictate which food is clean and unclean based on their sensorial experiences.[[243]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-243)
## Etiquette
Main article: [Etiquette of Indian dining](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Etiquette_of_Indian_dining "Etiquette of Indian dining")
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Eating_by_hands.jpg/250px-Eating_by_hands.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Eating_by_hands.jpg)Eating by hands
Traditionally, meals in India are eaten while seated either on the floor, or on very low stools or mattress. Food is most often eaten with the hands rather than cutlery. 
Often _[roti](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roti "Roti")_ is used to scoop [curry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curry "Curry") without allowing it to touch the hand. In the wheat-producing north, a piece of _roti_ is gripped with the thumb and middle finger and ripped off while holding the _roti_ down with the index finger. 
A somewhat different method is used in the south for _[dosai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dosa_\(food\) "Dosa \(food\)")_ , _adai_ , and _[uththappam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uttapam "Uttapam")_ , where the middle finger is pressed down to hold the bread and the forefinger and thumb used to grip and separate a small part. Traditional serving styles vary regionally throughout India. 
Contact with other cultures has affected Indian dining etiquette. For example, the Anglo-Indian middle class commonly uses spoons and forks, as is traditional in Western culture.[[244]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-GistWright1973-244)
In South India, cleaned [banana leaves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banana_leaves "Banana leaves"), which can be disposed of after meals, are used for serving food. When hot food is served on banana leaves, the leaves add distinctive aromas and taste to the food.[[245]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Dubey2011\(2\)-245) Leaf plates are less common today, except on special occasions. 
## Outside India
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Chicken_Tikka.jpg/250px-Chicken_Tikka.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Chicken_Tikka.jpg)_Chicken tikka_
Indian migration has spread the culinary traditions of the subcontinent throughout the world. These cuisines have been adapted to local tastes, and have also affected local cuisines. The international appeal of [curry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curry "Curry") has been compared to that of [pizza](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pizza "Pizza").[[246]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-246) Indian _[tandoor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tandoor "Tandoor")_ dishes such as _[chicken tikka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicken_tikka "Chicken tikka")_ also enjoy widespread popularity.[[247]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-247)
### Australia
A [Roy Morgan Research](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roy_Morgan_Research "Roy Morgan Research") survey taken between 2013 and 2018 found that Indian cuisine was the top-rated international food among 51% of Australians, behind Chinese, Italian, and Thai.[[248]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-248)
### Canada
As in the United Kingdom and the United States, Indian cuisine is widely available in [Canada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canada "Canada"), especially in the cities of [Toronto](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"),[[249]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Davidson2009-249) [Vancouver](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"),[[250]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Olson2009-250) and [Ottawa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa") where the majority of Canadians of [South Asian heritage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asian_Canadian "Asian Canadian") live. 
### China
Indian food is gaining popularity in [China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/China "China"), where there are many Indian restaurants in [Beijing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), [Shanghai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), and [Shenzhen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen"). [Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") alone has more than 50 Indian restaurants, some of which date back to the 1980s. Most of the Indian restaurants in Hong Kong are in [Tsim Sha Tsui](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tsim_Sha_Tsui "Tsim Sha Tsui").[[251]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Mathews2011-251)
### Fiji
[Indo-Fijians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indo-Fijians "Indo-Fijians") have a similar cuisine, often with [Fijian influence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fijian_cuisine "Fijian cuisine"). 
### Middle East
The Indian culinary scene in the [Middle East](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") has been influenced greatly by the large [Indian diaspora](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-resident_Indian_and_person_of_Indian_origin "Non-resident Indian and person of Indian origin") in these countries. Centuries of trade relations and cultural exchange resulted in a significant influence on each region's cuisines. The use of the _[tandoor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tandoor "Tandoor")_ , which originated in northwestern India,[[252]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Civitello2011-252) is an example. 
The large influx of Indian expatriates into Middle Eastern countries during the 1970s and 1980s led to a boom in Indian restaurants to cater to this population and was also widely influenced by the local and international cuisines. 
### Nepal
Indian cuisine is available in the streets of Nepalese cities, including [Kathmandu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kathmandu "Kathmandu") and [Janakpur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Janakpur "Janakpur"). 
### Southeast Asia
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Komala%27s_Restaurant%2C_Serangoon_Road%2C_Singapore_-_20060827.jpg/250px-Komala%27s_Restaurant%2C_Serangoon_Road%2C_Singapore_-_20060827.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Komala%27s_Restaurant,_Serangoon_Road,_Singapore_-_20060827.jpg)An Indian restaurant in [Singapore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singapore "Singapore")
Other cuisines which borrow inspiration from Indian cooking styles include [Cambodian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cambodian_cuisine "Cambodian cuisine"), [Lao](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lao_cuisine "Lao cuisine"), [Filipino](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Filipino_cuisine "Filipino cuisine"), [Vietnamese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vietnamese_cuisine "Vietnamese cuisine"), [Indonesian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesian_cuisine "Indonesian cuisine"), [Thai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thai_cuisine "Thai cuisine"), and [Burmese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Burmese_cuisine "Burmese cuisine") cuisines. The spread of vegetarianism in other parts of Asia is often credited to Hindu and Buddhist practices.[[253]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Corum2000-253)
Indian cuisine is very popular in [Southeast Asia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), due to the strong Hindu and Buddhist cultural influence in the region. Indian cuisine has had considerable influence on Malaysian cooking styles[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-vegvoyages.com-5) and also enjoys popularity in [Singapore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singapore "Singapore").[[254]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-254)[[255]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-255) There are numerous North and South Indian restaurants in Singapore, mostly in [Little India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Little_India,_Singapore "Little India, Singapore"). 
Singapore is also known for [fusion cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fusion_cuisine "Fusion cuisine") combining traditional [Singaporean cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singaporean_cuisine "Singaporean cuisine") with Indian influences. Fish head curry, for example, is a local creation. Indian influence on [Malay cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malay_cuisine "Malay cuisine") dates to the 19th century.[[256]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-256)
### United Kingdom
[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Chicken_Tikka_Masala.jpg/250px-Chicken_Tikka_Masala.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Chicken_Tikka_Masala.jpg)_Chicken tikka masala_ , a modified version of Indian _chicken tikka_ , has been called "a true British [national dish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_dish "National dish")."[[257]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Guardian1-257)
The UK's first Indian restaurant, the [Hindoostanee Coffee House](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindoostanee_Coffee_House "Hindoostanee Coffee House"), opened in 1810.[[258]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-BBC-258)[[259]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-:0-259) By 2003, there were as many as 10,000 restaurants serving Indian cuisine in [England](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/England "England") and [Wales](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wales "Wales") alone. According to [Britain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")'s [Food Standards Agency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Food_Standards_Agency "Food Standards Agency"), the Indian food industry in the United Kingdom is worth 3.2 billion [pounds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling"), accounts for two-thirds of all eating out in the country, and serves about 2.5 million customers every week.[[259]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-:0-259)
One of the best known examples of British Indian restaurant cuisine is _[chicken tikka masala](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicken_tikka_masala "Chicken tikka masala")_ , which has also been called "a true British national dish."[[260]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-Guardian-260)
### Ireland
Ireland's first Indian restaurant, the Indian Restaurant and Tea Rooms, opened in 1908 on Sackville Street, now [O'Connell Street](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/O%27Connell_Street "O'Connell Street"), in Dublin.[[261]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-261) Today, Indian restaurants are commonplace in most Irish cities and towns. Non-Chinese Asians are the fastest growing ethnic group in Ireland.[[262]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-262)
### United States
A survey by _[The Washington Post](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")_ in 2007 stated that more than 1,200 Indian food products had been introduced into the [United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States "United States") since 2000.[[263]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_cuisine#cite_note-263) There are numerous Indian restaurants across the US, which vary based on regional culture and climate. [North Indian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_India "North India") and [South Indian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_India "South India") cuisines are especially well represented. Most Indian restaurants in the United States serve [Americanised](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Americanization "Americanization") versions of North Indian food, which is generally less spicy than its Indian equivalents. 
At sit-down restaurants with [North Indian cuisine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_Indian_cuisine "North Indian cuisine") (the most common), complimentary _[papadum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Papadum "Papadum")_ is served with three dipping sauces—typically _hari chutney_ (mint and cilantro), _imli chutney_ (taramind), and a spicy red chilli or onion _[chutney](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chutney "Chutney")_ —in place of European-style bread before the meal.

# ranchi News
[RMC intensifies dog sterilisation, urges owners to register petsThe Ranchi Municipal Corporation is taking bold steps to reign in the stray dog numbers by amplifying sterilization and vaccination efforts. Residents are invited to register their pet dogs, which entails a nominal charge and necessary paperwork. This ongoing initiative reflects the civic body's commitment to responsibly managing the balance between stray dogs and household pets in the community.](https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/city/ranchi/rmc-intensifies-dog-sterilisation-urges-owners-to-register-pets/articleshow/129617745.cms)[Safe return of crew: LPG tanker Shivalik docks safely at Mundra; Family expresses relief after anxious week amidst West Asia conflictsAgainst the backdrop of missile strikes, Ansh Tripathy, a 34-year-old engineer, skillfully led his 26-member crew aboard the LPG tanker Shivalik through the treacherous Strait of Hormuz. The ship safely docked at Mundra port on Monday evening, dispelling days of anxiety for Ansh's family who had been glued to news reports on the escalating conflict in West Asia.](https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/city/ranchi/safe-return-of-crew-lpg-tanker-shivalik-docks-safely-at-mundra-family-expresses-relief-after-anxious-week-amidst-west-asia-conflicts/articleshow/129617724.cms)
